text:
OK so I just totalled the receipts for the chess board because Camila asked me out of curiosity, mostly, because she's seen me bleeding money out of the woodworking corner of our living room for three months and I think she wanted to know whether I'd accidentally bought a yacht. I dumped it all into a Google Sheet at like 1am tonight. Final number: ~3500 MXN in materials. That's it. Three thousand five hundred pesos for everything that ended up in the actual board. About 175 USD. I keep staring at the number because it does not feel proportionate to the amount of crying-at-2am-because-the-grain-is-wrong I did over the last 90 days. Let me actually break it down for you because I think the breakdown is funny and I want to write it down before I forget every line item the way I forget everything that isn't currently on fire.

Cedar planks from Maderería El Roble in Toluca — that drive I did back in month 14 with Pablo, the one where we got stuck behind a tortilla truck for 40 minutes coming back through Constituyentes — that was 1100 MXN for two 1.5m planks of clear, knot-free heartwood, plus another 220 MXN for a smaller piece of the same batch when I had to redo the frame after the grain orientation disaster. So 1320 MXN in cedar. Walnut for the dark squares — I bought those squares pre-cut from a small supplier in Iztapalapa that a guy on the Mexican Woodworkers Telegram pointed me to, 480 MXN for the set, sized to my dimensions. Magnetic discs for the piece bases (the little neodymium ones, 6mm), 280 MXN — and yes I overbought, I have like 40 spare magnets in a drawer, ask me how I know. PLA filament for the 3D-printed bases I prototyped before I went magnetic — 150 MXN, mostly wasted because the first design was hideous and the second design wasn't much better and the third was the magnet pivot anyway. Beeswax block for the finish — 90 MXN at a beekeeper's stand at the Mercado Roma, the same lady who sells the crystallized honey I love — and mineral oil to cut it with, 60 MXN. Sandpaper assortment from 80 grit up to 600 grit — 320 MXN, and I burned through most of it because I'm a beginner who oversands and re-sands and re-sands until the cedar starts looking like it's apologizing to me. Wood glue (Titebond III, the waterproof one — bought specifically because I read that it'd help with the warping and lol that didn't save me from the corner that cupped in month 16, but at least the re-glue held), 180 MXN. Clamps — and this is where the receipt gets embarrassing — 620 MXN because I bought four parallel-jaw clamps after the warp emergency and I will never stop using them, they are forever clamps, but in the moment Camila walked into the apartment and said órale, we have a clamp wall now? Painters tape, dust masks, a cheap caliper, miscellaneous bits and bobs — call it 200 MXN.

Adds up to roughly 3700 if I'm honest with the math, but I'm rounding down to 3500 because some of that beeswax is still in the block, and the spare magnets and the leftover clamps live on as tools, not as part of the board. Materials that are physically in the cedar object I'm gifting to Hugo on Saturday: ~3500 MXN.

What the number doesn't include — and what I'm now ruminating on at 1am because my brain won't shut off — is the Festool TS 55 REQ plunge router. That's 14k MXN by itself. The Veritas chisel set was another 4k. So if you count the tools, this is a 21k MXN board. If I were ever, god forbid, to try to sell this thing or even price it as an artisan would, I'd have to amortize that across whatever number of boards I think I'll make in the next 5 years, which is — let me check — currently zero, because I don't know if I'll ever make another one. The truth is the tools were a one-time gateway. Pablo keeps joking I should make him a cutting board to recoup the router cost. I told him to bring me cedar.

I also did not count gas for the round trips to Toluca (three of them, La Torta is fuel-efficient but not free, maybe 800 MXN of gas total), nor the fancy sharpening stone I bought for the chisels because YouTube told me to (1100 MXN, do not get me started on woodworking YouTube), nor the four pizzas Pablo and I ate during sanding sessions in our garage (he refuses to count those as material cost, says they were 'workshop labor compensation,' fine), nor the small box of band-aids I went through, nor the two packs of fine-grit sponge sanders I burned through trying to break the edges, nor the new dust collector hose I bought because the cheap one collapsed under shop-vac suction. If I were honest the all-in cost is closer to 5k. But that's tools and consumables, not materials.

What the number really doesn't include, and this is the soft thing I keep tripping over: the time. Three months of every Saturday for three to six hours, plus most Sunday mornings, plus a few evenings I sneaked in after Brújula stand-up. If I valued my time at my hourly Brújula rate (let's just say 95k USD eq divided by 2000 hours, ~47 USD/hr, ~800 MXN/hr) the chess board would be a six-figure object, which is the dumbest possible accounting and I include it only because Camila brought it up tonight as a joke. Her face when I started actually doing the math: priceless. She said 'Sebastián you do not need to assign a billable rate to your hobby, this is what hobbies are for.' Fair.

The spreadsheet has one column at the bottom that I added on a whim — 'value to recipient (estimated).' I left it blank. Because the truth is I have no idea what this thing is worth to Hugo. He's 70 years old on Saturday. His wife died eighteen months ago. His daughter — my Camila — is going to hand him a wrapped object after dinner and he's going to open it and the value of that moment is going to be — I genuinely cannot price it. I cannot even guess at the order of magnitude. The 3500 number is the only honest number I have, and the 3500 number is barely related to the actual answer.

The other thing I keep coming back to is the cost relative to other things I've spent 3500 pesos on this year. 3500 is roughly: a single nice dinner for the two of us at one of those tasting-menu places in Polanco. Or two months of yerba mate consumption. Or three months of Atlas Box dues. Or a third of one month of the new rent in Roma Norte. The chess board is the same nominal cost as one fancy dinner. The chess board is going to outlive me. I will be in the ground and there's a non-zero chance Camila and I will have a kid who will inherit Hugo's chess board from his grandfather and will pass it to their kid and there will be another forty years of someone playing the Italian opening on cedar wood I bought in Toluca. The dinner would be gone in 90 minutes. The economics of artisanal objects are deranged when you compare them to consumable objects. We pay for memory storage in physical form and we don't even know we're doing it.

Anyway. 3500 in actual materials. Hugo cannot know this number. He cannot know any number. The whole point of the gift is that it looks like it cost ten times more than it did. Camila said tonight, looking at the spreadsheet over my shoulder, 'mi amor, the value of this board is not in pesos.' Which is what she always says when she wants me to stop spreadsheet-ing my feelings. She's right. I'm going to close the spreadsheet. The board's drying its final beeswax coat tonight on the dining table — I rubbed in the third coat this morning and the cedar is starting to glow that warm amber-gold I was hoping for — and I am going to not touch it again until Saturday morning when I wrap it in linen and hide it in the closet. Just need this last 36 hours to not screw anything up. I have a bad habit of over-fiddling with finished work and I have explicitly told Camila to stop me if she sees me reach for the sandpaper one more time. She has agreed. She also said 'you absolutely will reach for the sandpaper anyway' which is rude and accurate.

One more thing while I'm at it. The brass plaque on the underside — I haven't told anyone about this. I had it engraved at a tiny shop on Calle Orizaba two weeks ago. It says 'Hugo, 70 — Sebastián, 2026.' That's it. No flourishes. The guy at the engraving shop wanted to add 'con cariño' or 'feliz cumpleaños' and I said no, just the names and the year. Hugo will see it when he flips the board over to set up pieces. The plaque cost 280 MXN. I did include that in the 3500 number. It might be the most important 280 MXN on the receipt.

The board itself, by the way, is 20 inches square, 1.75 inches thick, 32 walnut squares inlaid into the cedar with maybe 1mm of variance from perfect (I measured) which is honestly miraculous given that I had never operated a plunge router before September. The piece set has 32 magnetic chess pieces with cedar bases (matching the board) and printed plastic toppers I designed in Fusion 360 over four weekends. The pieces store in a fitted recess on the side that I cut with a forstner bit on the third try (the first two recess attempts had to be sanded out and started over). Total weight: about 4 kilos. Hugo will not be able to lift it casually. He will have to commit to picking it up. I think that's correct for an heirloom object. Heirlooms should ask something of you when you handle them. The board asks four kilos.  I keep thinking about the 280 MXN brass plaque. Tiny number. Outsized importance. The plaque is the most concentrated meaning per peso of anything in the cedar object. The Festool router is 14000 MXN of meaning per object. The plaque is 280 MXN of meaning per gesture. The exchange rate is wild. Hugo will look at the plaque tomorrow night, see his name and mine and the year, and it will be the most expensive 280 pesos in the entire room. Camila pointed this out tonight when I showed her the engraving. She said I was getting weird about the math again. She is right. The math has stopped being literal. The math has become its own kind of poem.

 The other thought I keep returning to: Hugo will set the chess board up in his apartment in São Paulo and play through openings on it on rainy mornings. The cedar will absorb a different humidity than CDMX. The grain will move. The board will warp differently than it did here. I should send him a small bottle of mineral oil with a note about how often to feed the surface. Maybe twice a year. Maybe in the rainy seasons specifically. The board needs maintenance for the next forty years and I want to make sure he knows what kind. I will write the maintenance note on Sunday. The note will go on the underside in pencil so he can read it once and forget it but the board will remember.

Email from MIT admissions just landed and qué onda, the GRE waiver got granted. They sent the form letter that says 'based on your professional experience and academic record, the standardized test requirement is waived for your application.' That's it, no further qualifications, just a one-line approval. I'd been sweating this for three weeks because the program officially says GRE optional but recommended-if-you-haven't-published, and I haven't published anything other than a Brújula post-mortem and a few Mastodon threads about Tokio runtime starvation. Ten years out of school plus a senior IC role at a Series B was apparently enough. Honestly the waiver shaves about a month off my prep time and saves me 220 dollars and the indignity of redoing GRE math after a decade. I texted Camila immediately. She replied with five fire emojis and 'no te confíes' which is exactly the right amount of encouragement. Now I have no excuse not to finish the personal statement. There was a small dishonest part of me that was hoping the waiver would get rejected so I could shelf the whole thing for a cycle. That part of me has been overruled by the part of me that has been dreaming about Picard's lab for six months. Onward. I texted Pablo too — he replied 'pendejo good' which is his highest form of praise. I texted my mom and she replied with three crying-laughing emojis and a voice memo that I cannot bring myself to listen to until I have more emotional capacity. I texted Daniela, who replied 'told you' even though she had not, in fact, told me that. Something about a GRE waiver is a small bureaucratic blessing — a stranger at MIT looked at my file and decided I do not need to suffer one more standardized test. Whoever they are, gracias. The waiver email is now starred and pinned in my inbox like it's a holy relic. I will probably print it. I might frame it. Camila said do not frame it, it is not a diploma, please do not frame it. Fine, no frame. But I am still printing it.

 I forwarded the waiver email to my dad too. He took twenty minutes to reply and when he did he just said 'aprovecha, mijo' which is the Mexican-dad version of 'go for it' and which from him carries about ten times the weight of any other phrasing. He almost never volunteers career-encouragement. Three Christmases ago he told me, with characteristic understatement, that he thought my Brújula promotion was 'a respectable step' and I held that one in my chest for two months. The 'aprovecha, mijo' is bigger than that. He wouldn't say so out loud. Mom will translate it for him later. The other thing the waiver does — and this is the part I haven't quite metabolized — is it makes the application feel administratively real. The optional steps are gone. The form is shorter. The deadline is the deadline. There's no other test left to schedule, no extra study window to invoke as a reason to delay. The path is open. The only thing standing between me and the submit button is the personal statement and the rec letters. Both of which are in motion. Both of which I have been making excuses about. The waiver removed an excuse. The waiver was secretly the last excuse. Now there are no excuses. Now there is just the work. Camila brought me chamomile tea while I was rereading the email for the fourth time and she said 'now there is no excuse' and she was reading my mind. Married minds. Or whatever the cohabitation-equivalent of married minds is.  I taped the printed waiver letter to the wall above my desk between the Brújula post-mortem one-pager I keep there as a personal trophy and a printout of the GitHub stars graph for the PostGIS crate. The wall is a small museum of unlikely wins. Camila walked past tonight and saw the new addition and said 'the museum grows.' She is going to charge admission soon.

Just now realising the waiver also means the application timeline is actually real. Like the deadline is a real number on a real calendar. December 1. I'd been treating it as theoretical. Now it's not theoretical. Now it's six weeks away. Now it's the only thing I can think about when I close my eyes. December 1 is no longer a hypothetical Wednesday. It is a Wednesday with a button on it. I am going to be unbearable to live with for six weeks.

OK so I sat down tonight and actually drafted the whole MIT app end-to-end. Not the personal statement final, but everything else — the form fields, the CV upload, the research interests text box (250 words, kept it tight), the diversity statement (I really debated whether to discuss the Mexican-engineer-pivoting-to-academia angle, decided yes, kept it grounded), the funding question (no, I am not requesting funding for first year, hoping to convert later), the prior advisors list, the publications field (left blank, with a sentence in the personal statement explaining why), all of it. Took me about 4 hours straight, no bathroom breaks, two yerba mates. I have a PDF preview saved. Personal statement is the only thing not in final form — currently sitting at draft 5, will probably keep going. The actual application is now a single button-press away once the statement is locked. Felt strange to look at the rendered preview and see my name attached to a 'Media Lab — Affective Computing' admissions packet. Like an out-of-body moment. Hi yes hello, I am applying. To MIT. Apparently. I keep wondering what 22-year-old me at ITESM in 2014 would say if he saw this PDF. He'd probably tell me to lock it in. He'd also ask why it took 12 years. I don't have a good answer for him. I have a long answer that involves Wizeline and Brújula and a postgres outage but no, no good answer. The CV upload took me 90 minutes alone because I kept second-guessing whether to include the kickboxing instructor certification (no — irrelevant), whether to list the PostGIS crate as a publication (yes, in the 'open-source contributions' section), and whether to include my high-school robotics medal from 2009 (god no, what was I thinking). The research interests text box was the easiest part because I'd been writing variants of that paragraph in the personal statement for six weeks. I finally pasted in a clean 240-word version and was done in 15 minutes. The whole packet now lives on the ThinkPad in a folder called 'mit_app_final/' which is overconfident naming but here we are. The portal-side draft is saved every 5 minutes automatically. There is no excuse for losing work. Camila keeps asking when I'm submitting. I keep saying 'after one more pass.' She has stopped asking.  One small thing I forgot to mention — the application portal has this little progress meter that fills up section by section as you complete fields. I watched it tick from 70% to 100% over the four hours and it was, embarrassingly, very satisfying. Game-design dopamine applied to grad-school admissions. I do not know who designed the MIT portal but I would like to shake their hand.

I want to think out loud about the personal statement angle because every time I sit down to refine it I keep flip-flopping and I want to lock it in tonight, or at least lock down the conceptual frame so I stop moving sideways and start moving forward.

The theme — what I keep coming back to — is ML for nonverbal feedback in physical training. That's the one-sentence version. The longer version is: I want to study how machine learning systems can give athletes (and patients, and physical-therapy users, and anyone learning a body skill) real-time, non-judgmental feedback on the parts of motion that are hard to self-perceive. Things like hip rotation in a roundhouse kick, weight distribution in a stance, the timing of a chamber. Things a coach screams at you and you nod and you still cannot feel until 200 reps later. The hypothesis I want to put in writing — even though it's vibes-based right now — is that affective computing as a discipline has spent a lot of effort on detecting human emotional state from face/voice, and not nearly enough on closing the loop, on giving humans back proprioceptive information they can't otherwise access. The body is a black box from the inside, especially when you're learning. ML can crack the box open without a coach having to be there.

I know this is the angle because it's the one project I worked on this whole arc that I was working on out of want, not need. The pose-estimation thing started because I wanted to know, after 15 months as a blue belt, whether my left hook was actually rotating my hips or whether Profe was being polite. TensorFlow was too slow on the M2's CPU when I tried to do real-time analysis without a GPU, so I burned a few weeks pivoting to MediaPipe Pose, which gave me 30+ FPS on phone-shot 1080p video. Even in its current paused state on my GitHub I have like 12 sparring sessions logged and Pablo plus three other gym friends ran through it. The system told me my chamber timing was inconsistent — you don't snap the leg back fast enough on the second kick of a combo — and I'd been getting that exact note from Profe for two years, and somehow seeing the graph next to the video unlocked it for me in a way the verbal feedback never did. That's the seed. The personal statement is basically: I lived this. Now I want to study it.

The risk in the framing is that it sounds gimmicky — 'oh he wants to do affective computing because of his hobby.' I keep trying to reframe it so the kickboxing is illustrative, not the point. The point is the broader question of nonverbal sensorimotor feedback. Kickboxing happens to be the domain I have data on, but the application surface is rehabilitation, motor-skill acquisition in stroke recovery, surgical training, music education. The angle Picard's group has historically taken with affective computing is emotional state, but there's a thread in her later work — and I cited two papers in my draft — about extending affective computing to bodily self-knowledge. That's the thread I'm trying to pull.

The other thing I keep wrestling with is the autobiographical-vs-research-pitch ratio. Draft 1 was way too autobiographical. I had a paragraph about earning my black belt and Profe handing it to me and how that moment crystallized why I cared. Draft 3 went the opposite way, all research questions and citations, and Camila read it and said 'this could be anyone, it's not you.' Draft 5 is somewhere in the middle. I open with the body-as-black-box framing, drop one paragraph about the pose-est project as evidence I will actually do the work, then pivot fast into the research questions. I think that's the shape. I'll know in another two drafts whether the shape holds.

The other-other thing I keep wrestling with is whether to mention rehabilitation as the primary application surface or as a secondary application surface. Primary feels more dignified — 'I want to help stroke patients' — but it also feels like I'm reaching for the morally weighty version of the project to dress up what is really, fundamentally, a sports-science interest. Secondary feels more honest. The truth is I came to this question through kickboxing and I have to be honest that kickboxing is the data I have, and the rehabilitation framing is the natural extension that makes the work matter beyond the dojo. Camila's argument is that admissions readers will read 'rehabilitation' and check the box and stop reading. Pablo's argument is that nobody believes you when you lead with rehabilitation if your actual data is sparring footage. I think I'll mention rehabilitation in one sentence, in the third paragraph, as the natural extension, and let kickboxing be the load-bearing example. That's the honesty calibration I can live with.

Third thing I'm wrestling with — and this is more tactical — is the Picard-citation ratio. The personal statement is 1000 words. If I cite three Picard papers I look like I'm sucking up. If I cite zero I look like I haven't read the work. Two feels right. One paper from her early canonical work to show I know the foundation, one from her recent work to show I'm tracking where the field is going. I think I have the two. Need to verify the year on the second.

Fourth thing I'm wrestling with — and Camila pointed this out last night — is whether the statement reads as someone who has been thinking about this problem for years, or as someone who decided last month that this is the angle and is now post-rationalizing. The honest answer is in between. The seed of the question goes back at least to the pose-est project in month 4. The crystallization of the question into 'this is the masters I want to apply for' came around month 15-16. So 15 months of incubation, maybe 4 months of formalization. I tried to write that timeline subtly into the second paragraph — using the phrase 'over the past 18 months' instead of 'recently' — but I'm not sure it lands. Camila says it lands. Pablo says it doesn't matter. I'm leaving it.

Fifth thing — and this is the thing I keep avoiding — the closer paragraph. The 'why now' question. I'm 33. Most masters applicants are 23. I have to address the age, gracefully, in maybe two sentences, without sounding apologetic. Currently the draft just says 'I have nine years of industry behind me, a clear research interest, and the time and willingness to start over in a research context.' Camila says it's fine. Pablo says it's fine. I think I'm overthinking it. I'm going to leave it and move on.

Sixth — and possibly final — thing. The opening sentence. Currently 'The body is a black box from the inside.' Camila reads this as profound. Pablo reads it as faintly philosophy-grad-student-y. The compromise I keep almost-reaching is to start with a specific sensory detail — closing my eyes during a kick combination, trying to feel my hip angle, failing — and then hand-off to the abstraction in the second paragraph. That structure works in essays generally. The risk is that it reads as cute. The benefit is that it grounds the statement in a body, which is what the statement is about. I think I'll try the sensory-opening version as draft 6 and see how it reads.

Going to do draft 6 tonight. Goal: 1000 words, locked frame, kick-with-eyes-closed opening, one project paragraph, three research-question paragraphs, one why-MIT paragraph, one closer. Then I sleep on it and read it again Wednesday morning. If draft 6 holds I'll do a polish-pass for draft 7 and submit. If draft 6 falls apart I will write draft 7 from scratch. Either way: this is the last week. I'm submitting before Friday or I'm not submitting this cycle. Forcing function. I have told Camila to hold me to it. She has agreed and is already bracing for my Thursday-night meltdown.

 Seventh thing I keep avoiding — the question of my own publication record. I have none. Zero peer-reviewed papers. Zero workshop papers. The closest things I have are: a Brújula post-mortem doc that's internal and which I've been allowed to share publicly with names redacted; a series of Mastodon threads about Tokio runtime starvation that got picked up by a few rust-async folks I follow; the PostGIS encoder open-source crate which has 87 GitHub stars and four production users; a few blog posts on a dev blog that's basically my journal. None of that is real publication. The personal statement has to acknowledge this honestly without dwelling on it. The Picard-style approach to advisor letters is to look for evidence of intellectual seriousness, not just credential symmetry. The PostGIS crate is evidence. The pose-est project is evidence. The post-mortems are evidence. The personal statement has to point at these as evidence and let them speak. If the reader concludes I'm worth taking a chance on, they conclude that. If not, the publication record won't save me anyway. Eighth thing — and this might actually be the last — the structural question of whether I should mention the second author on the pose-est project. There is no second author. Pablo was the test user, the gym friends were test users, but I wrote every line of code myself. The personal statement currently says 'I built' which is honest. Camila asked whether I should soften it to 'I led the development of' which sounds more professional. I refuse. 'I built' is the truth. If MIT thinks 'I built' is gauche they can not admit me and they'll be wrong. The voice is the voice. Camila agreed when I pushed back. She said she was just testing whether I had a backbone. She has Pablo-lite tendencies. I love her for this. Going to sleep on the draft now. 12:47am. Back at it tomorrow at 6am with mate.  Final thing — the question of length. The form says under 1000 words. My current draft is 1023. Trim pass tomorrow will get it to 994. Six trim passes are baked in. The cuts are not arbitrary; each cut serves a sentence. I have a small spreadsheet in the side document tracking which words I cut and why. Most cuts are adverbs and connective tissue. The hard cuts are the sentences I love that don't earn their seat. Camila has read both versions and has only flagged one cut as regrettable, which I overruled because the sentence was load-bearing for tone. The cuts are converging. The draft is converging. Friday I submit. Or Thursday if my will breaks early.

 One additional consideration — the question of whether to acknowledge previous attempts elsewhere. I have not applied to any other masters program this cycle. I considered Carnegie Mellon's HCII briefly and Stanford's Symbolic Systems briefly and decided neither fit the specific question I want to study. The personal statement does not mention this. The personal statement just mentions MIT. The omission is honest but it could be read as either single-minded focus or naive overconfidence. I think single-minded focus is closer to the truth, given the specificity of my research questions. Picard's group is uniquely positioned. Other programs would be wrong-shaped. The personal statement implies this rather than stating it.

Spent tonight with Camila working out logistics for Hugo's party Saturday. He flies in tomorrow afternoon — AM 138 from GRU, lands 6:40pm. We're picking him up in La Torta. Camila will not let me play any of my Latin jazz on the drive home from the airport because she says her dad has had enough Beny Moré in his life and would prefer silence. Fine. The party is Saturday at 7, here at the apartment, about 18 people: my parents flying in from Guadalajara (mom Isabela insisted on bringing her own pan dulce, do not even try to stop her), my brother Mateo and Sofía driving down from Tijuana to make a long weekend of it, three of Hugo's old engineering colleagues from a mining-survey company he worked with in the 90s who are now all retired in CDMX, plus a handful of Camila's CDMX architecture friends. It's a lot for our 5th-floor walk-up. The chess board needs to be wrapped, hidden in the closet, and presented after dinner before the cake. I keep mentally rehearsing where I'll set it down so the unveiling looks deliberate. Camila vetoed my plan to do a small speech beforehand because she said I would 'find a way to mention the wood grain,' which is rude but accurate. New plan: silent presentation. Hugo opens it. The room reacts. I stand in the back. Done. Catering — I'm cooking the entrée side because I refuse to outsource the food at our own apartment, and Camila is doing appetizers and the cake. Total food cost ~2200 MXN. I'm doing roasted poblano pasta with my sauce, a portobello mole, and a wild rice thing. Camila vetoed cilantro across the board because Hugo and I share the soap gene. We bought four bottles of mezcal and three of wine. Doña Marta has been warned that there will be a party Saturday night and she's allowed to come knock on the door if it gets too loud. She declined politely and said she'll just go to bed early. Doña Marta is the saint of this building. I'm going to bring her a slice of pan dulce in the morning.

 The other thing weighing on me — and I keep forgetting to write it down — is whether to do dad-and-Hugo introductions in front of everyone or quietly off to the side. They've met four times in five years but always in transit, always in airports or hotel lobbies, never with a beat. They're both retired teachers (well, Hugo's an engineer who taught for a decade post-retirement, dad is a high-school physics teacher who's been retired three years now), both in their late 60s/early 70s, both fans of Latin jazz, both have the cilantro soap gene as I just learned tonight, and both think their respective daughters/sons-in-law are the cat's pajamas. They will get along. The question is choreography. Camila said do not orchestrate, just let them find each other. I am going to obey. I am going to put the playlist on, set out the appetizers, refill the mezcal glasses, and let the two old men find their own way to a corner of the living room where they will discuss whatever it is that retired Mexican high-school physics teachers and retired Brazilian civil engineers find to discuss. Camila says the answer is 'their grandchildren who don't yet exist' and I told her shut up. She laughed. Then she said it again because she's like that. The party is going to be a small miracle of Latin American family logistics and I have been overthinking my role in it for a week.  Camila put together a small playlist for the moment Hugo walks into the apartment from the airport. Three Brazilian songs from the 70s — Caetano, Chico, Elis — that she remembers playing in his house when she was a child. He will not consciously notice the playlist but he will feel it. That's the design. The apartment will smell like roasted poblano and sound like Brasília 1976. Hugo will be home.

going to bed. doña marta starts the oven in like 4 hours. let's see if i actually sleep tonight or if it's another 3am special. pre-party nerves on top of the application nerves on top of the chess board nerves. layered nervous system. nube is on the bed already. i'm going to put my phone in the other room. that's the rule i've been failing for months. tonight i'm going to actually do it. Camila is brushing her teeth. The kitchen is mostly clean. The chess board is wrapped in linen in the closet. The food is in the fridge in tupperwares labelled with masking-tape sharpie because I'm that guy now. The mezcal is on the shelf where I can see it but not reach it without intent. Tomorrow at 6:40pm Hugo lands. Tomorrow at 7pm Saturday is the party. Today between now and then is a 36-hour stretch I want to spend asleep, more asleep, and then a quick walk with Nube. The phone is in the other room. The laptop is in the

Saturday night now. Party's wrapping. I want to log this while it's fresh because the next 24 hours are going to be a sleep-coma and then back to work and I'll lose the texture if I don't write it down.

Hugo's 70th birthday party — held tonight in our apartment in Roma Norte, CDMX. Eighteen people, packed into our 80-square-meter 2BR like sardines. Camila moved every piece of furniture against the wall yesterday morning and we still ended up with people sitting on the floor against the bookshelf. The two-continent thing was the wild part. Hugo flew in from São Paulo Thursday, my parents and Mateo and Sofía drove and flew in from the Mexico side, and three of Hugo's old work colleagues — civil engineers he hadn't seen in 25 years, all in CDMX now, all 70+ — showed up wearing nearly identical pressed shirts and bringing nearly identical bottles of mezcal. It looked like a uniform. They started singing 'Las Mañanitas' at 9pm and didn't stop for 20 minutes. They knew every verse. My dad — who is 66 and carries the angina around like a small bag he keeps having to put down — joined in on the chorus and I watched my mom watch him with that look she's had since the bypass, half-pride and half-vigilance. It's not subtle. It hasn't been subtle for four years.

The food situation: I made the entrée side, Camila handled appetizers and dessert. I did a roasted poblano pasta with my own sauce — yes, the sauce, the one I've been refining for two years — plus a pumpkin-seed mole over portobellos and a wild rice thing with charred scallions. Vegetarian-host special. I had a mild panic at 5pm when I realized one of Hugo's engineer friends had told us at the door he 'doesn't really do mushrooms,' but he ate three portions and told Camila in Portuguese that whoever made the food was a wizard. I didn't correct him. Hugo specifically requested I not put cilantro in anything. He knows about my soap gene and apparently he has it too — turns out it runs through the Ferreira side. We compared notes for ten minutes and I felt seen. Camila stood there shaking her head while we listed the dishes our mothers had ruined for us with cilantro garnish.

My mom and Camila spent forty minutes in the kitchen at one point, Camila showing her the pan dulce molds she'd bought for the cake, my mom showing her a technique for the dough where you fold the butter in cold instead of softened. They were speaking a hybrid of Mexican Spanish and Camila's slow Brazilian Portuguese and my mom's terrible English fragments and I stayed out of the kitchen because I didn't want to break the spell. This is the marriage we don't talk about — the families learning each other on a Saturday night in our small apartment. I caught myself thinking, briefly, about kid-timing. Camila and I had agreed not to talk about it tonight. We didn't. But I watched my mom's hands on the dough and I watched Camila watching my mom's hands on the dough and the kid-timing question was sitting there in the kitchen with us, just unverbalized.

Mateo got a little drunk on mezcal and at one point pulled me into the hallway and asked me, with that earnest face he gets, whether I think Sofía is the one. I told him brother, you proposed already, that ship has sailed. He laughed and then his face fell and he said 'no, I mean really.' We talked for ten minutes in the hallway with the bathroom door cracked behind us and the party noise leaking through the walls and I told him: yeah, I think she is. I think you know. He nodded, hugged me, and walked back to her like nothing had happened. Sofía glanced at me from across the room when he sat back down. She didn't look worried. She looked like someone who knows she got asked about and was happy about it.

Dad didn't drink. Just water. He told my mom in front of me that the angina has been quiet for a few weeks and she told him she doesn't believe him until the next checkup. They've been married 38 years. They argue like a long-married couple — there's no heat in it.

Three of Hugo's old engineer friends — Don Federico, Don Ricardo, and a third whose name I never quite catch because he speaks fast Tampico-accented Spanish — sat in a corner of the living room for two hours and told stories about a 1994 mining-survey job in Sonora where Hugo had famously refused to sign off on a structural beam that the foreman wanted to certify. The story got better the more mezcal they drank. Apparently Hugo had threatened to walk off the site. Apparently the company had backed down. Apparently the beam had failed three years later in a way that proved Hugo right. Camila had heard the story before but not from these three at the same time and her face during the telling was something I want to remember — the half-smile of a daughter who has learned, in real time, that her father was the kind of engineer who refused to sign things. Hugo just sat there and smiled into his drink. Didn't correct them. Didn't add details.

Nube was a hero of the night. She was so over-stimulated by 8pm that she just hid under the dining table for an hour, and then around 9 she came out and started making rounds, accepting head-pats from each new person in turn like she was the official greeter. Hugo specifically loved her. He kept calling her in Portuguese 'pequena lobinha' which means 'little wolf' and I have been trying for six months to teach Camila to call her that and she refuses. Apparently it was always going to take Hugo to make it stick. Nube now responds to 'pequena lobinha' as well as her own name. I do not know how to feel about this.

The music was Camila's choice. She vetoed Beny Moré (citing previous correspondence about her father's Beny Moré exhaustion), so we ended up with a playlist that was about 60% bossa nova, 30% Mexican boleros, and 10% Mateo's chaotic insertions of late-90s reggaeton when he thought nobody was watching. The bossa nova was perfect for dinner. The boleros were perfect for the singing portion. The reggaeton, when it hit, made my mom dance for a full minute before she realized what she was doing and stopped, blushing. It was the highlight of the dinner that no one had planned.

The Nudo guys — yeah I asked them — the owners of my favorite ramen place at Av. Álvaro Obregón, who I'm friends with at this point, sent over a small container of their house chili oil as a 'feliz cumpleaños' gift for Hugo because Camila had mentioned the party there on Wednesday and they remembered. Hugo loved it. He put it on everything. He's taking the bottle back to São Paulo. The Nudo guys have just exported their first chili oil to Brazil.

The gift moment is its own log entry, I'm going to write that one separately. But I'll just say the room got very quiet at one specific point tonight and I will remember it for a long time.

It's 11:40pm now. The last guests just left. Hugo is in our guest room. Camila's brushing her teeth. Nube is asleep on the rug, exhausted from being told 'no' to chorizo about 14 times. The apartment is a disaster. There is mezcal on the bookshelf. Someone — and I suspect it was Don Federico — knocked over a small ceramic dish in the bathroom and tried to glue it back together with what looks like a piece of gum. Tomorrow is for cleaning. Tonight was — yeah.

 Doña Marta is going to wake up in 4 hours and start the bread oven and the smell is going to come up through the courtyard and into our kitchen and Hugo is going to wake up to the smell of fresh pan dulce on his first morning back in Mexico after his daughter's mother-in-law's funeral arc concluded eighteen months ago. He is going to think his life is good. The smell is going to do work that no human conversation can do. I love this building. I love Doña Marta. I have not told her about Hugo's birthday but I have left a note on the door asking her to come knock tomorrow morning to let me know when the first batch is out so I can buy a dozen for breakfast. She'll do it. She does this kind of favor. The Roma Norte building network is a soft net I do not deserve. We have lived here three years and we know everyone on our block by first name. The carnicero on Av. Álvaro Obregón. The fruit lady at the corner of Jalapa. The barista at Cardinal. The mechanic who fixes La Torta when it eats its alternator every 18 months. Doña Marta in the building. Mario at the bakery counter. The Nudo guys. Beto at the dry cleaner. The little flower stand at the metro entrance. We have built a small world. Hugo walked into it tonight and it received him. That's what good neighborhoods do. They receive your father-in-law on a random October weekend and feed him and sing to him and tell stories about beams that should not have been signed. The Roma Norte we live in does this. Other neighborhoods don't. I am very lucky to live here. Camila and I have talked, in the abstract, about whether we'd stay if Brújula went sour or if we needed a bigger place. The answer keeps being yes, we stay, we just buy a place a few blocks over if we have to. The neighborhood is the asset. The apartment is replaceable. The neighborhood is not. Tonight made that case for me again. I will write this down so I do not forget it the next time the rent goes up. The neighborhood is the asset.  Last thing tonight. The way Hugo and my dad found each other was actually pretty quiet — they sat together on the small couch by the bookcase for about 40 minutes after dinner, drinking water and mezcal respectively, and they talked in a mixture of slow Portuguese and slow Spanish about a textbook they had both used in their respective educations in the 1970s. They had a single book in common across two countries forty years apart. Camila pointed it out to me afterward in bed. She said her dad had not had that kind of long conversation with a stranger in years. The book in common was a translation of a French classical mechanics textbook that apparently the entire Latin American 1970s technical-education cohort had used. Identical chapters. Identical worked problems. The two old men compared the chapter on rotational dynamics for ten minutes. I love this story. I want to remember it.

 Final thought before I close my eyes. The party tonight is going to be remembered as the night Hugo cried over a chess board. I do not yet know that yet, of course — that beat is tomorrow night — but the whole evening was building toward something that would crack the politeness of an apartment full of relatives and old friends. The chess board is the crack. The room knows the crack is coming. Camila knows. I know. Hugo doesn't know. He is asleep upstairs. He is going to wake up to the smell of Doña Marta's bread and a CDMX morning and a daughter who has been waiting eighteen months to see his face do this thing tomorrow. The setup is in place. The pay-off is twenty hours away. Going to sleep.

OK I have to write the gift moment down now because it's been replaying in my head for the last hour and I don't want to lose any of it.

We'd finished dinner. The pasta was demolished, the mole portobello dish was scraped to glass, and the third bottle of mezcal had been opened over my mild objection. Camila stood up and tapped a glass and made a small speech about her dad — about how he'd lost Beatriz almost a year and a half ago, about how he'd moved through that loss with a quiet dignity that has changed the way she thinks about love and aging, about how every monthly visit since has been a small gift to her. She didn't cry. Her voice got thin in two places. Hugo was sitting on the chair we'd dragged in from the bedroom (we don't own a real dining chair count of 18, we improvise) and he had his hands folded in his lap and was looking at her like she was eight years old. He had on the dark blue shirt Beatriz bought him for his 65th. I noticed that because Camila had pointed it out to me earlier, when he came out of the guest room ready for the party, and her face had done something I won't describe.

Then Camila looked at me and nodded and I went to the closet and brought out the chess board.

I'd wrapped it in linen — Camila's idea, she said paper was wrong — tied with butcher's twine. It was heavier than people expected. I set it on the table in front of Hugo and stepped back. He untied the twine and folded back the linen and the room actually went silent. Cedar, beeswax-finished, the walnut squares dark against the lighter cedar grain, the magnetic pieces in their little fitted recess at the side, the small brass plaque on the underside that says 'Hugo, 70 — Sebastián, 2026' which I'd added two weeks ago because I'd hated all the fancier engraving options and that's what I'd want if it were me.

He didn't say anything for about eight seconds. He just looked at it. He ran his thumb over the edge — the corner I'd had to re-glue and clamp, the one that warped in month 16, you literally cannot tell, I checked it a thousand times — and then his face went, and he started crying. Not big crying. The way old men cry, where their face just kind of folds in on itself and the breath gets caught and tears come out without any sound. He kept his hand on the board.

My dad — and I will remember this until I die — my dad stood up, walked over, put his hand on Hugo's shoulder and said 'es hermoso, hermano.' Two grandfathers-in-law who have met four times in five years, hand on shoulder. Hugo nodded. Couldn't speak. Camila was crying. Mateo was filming on his phone, which I gave him the side-eye for and he stopped after about three seconds because he was crying too. Don Federico in the corner had his hand over his mouth. Sofía was holding Mateo's arm. Nube, sensing the energy shift, came over and put her chin on Hugo's knee, which broke a little of the tension, because Hugo laughed wetly and rubbed her ear.

He finally said, in Portuguese, 'Beatriz would have loved this.' That was the line. That was when I lost it. I had to walk to the kitchen and pretend to check on something. I stood at the sink for about ninety seconds trying to get my face back, holding onto the counter, breathing through it. Camila found me there a minute later and didn't say anything, just leaned her forehead on my shoulder. We stood like that for what was probably another ninety seconds. The party noise came back in pieces from the other room — someone laughing, someone clinking a glass, Hugo's voice resuming in Portuguese asking Mateo something about the magnets in the pieces.

When I came back to the living room Hugo was already setting up a starting position on the board, opening with white. He plays the Italian. He had a knight in his hand. He set it down on its magnetic base and the satisfying small click of it landing on the cedar made him laugh again. He looked up at me and said in his slow careful Portuguese (he switches when he wants to be precise) that the magnets were a wonderful touch because his hands shake a little now and he has been losing pieces between moves on his old wooden board. He said this matter-of-factly. I had not designed the magnets for this reason. I had designed the magnets because I'd seen a video of a guy on a train playing chess on a magnetic travel board and thought it was clever. The accidental usefulness of the magnets to a 70-year-old with mild tremor — I cannot tell you how much that broke me. I had to hold a glass of water for two minutes and not say anything.

He asked about the wood. He asked where it came from. Camila told him about the drive to Toluca. He nodded slowly. He turned the board over and saw the brass plaque on the underside and ran his thumb over my name and his name and the year. He said, in Portuguese still, 'I will play on this board until I cannot play anymore. And then I will give it to my grandson.' Hugo does not have a grandson. The 'my grandson' was a future-tense statement, the way old men talk about future grandchildren as already-real. Camila and I both heard it. We both did not look at each other when we heard it.

Don Federico from the corner asked Hugo to set up a quick game and Hugo did. They played the opening of an Italian. Hugo's hands were trembling slightly but the magnets held. Don Federico made a comment about the weight of the pieces — 'estos sí son piezas, hermano' — and Hugo nodded and said 'feitas pelo meu filho' which means 'made by my son.' Made by my son. He has used the word 'son' for me before, in passing, casually, the way you might say 'my Camila's partner.' He has never used it like that. He used it like that tonight, in Portuguese, in front of his old Mexican engineering friends, twelve hours after I had given him a chess board.

Three months. The wrong-grain-orientation re-plane. The 1am drives back from Toluca. The router I had to YouTube how to use. The corner that warped and the four parallel-jaw clamps. The 3500 MXN of cedar and walnut and beeswax. All of it bought that one moment. I would do it again tomorrow. I might actually do it again. I might make one for my own dad next year.

Later, when most of the guests had left and Hugo had gone to bed, my dad came over to where I was washing dishes and stood next to me and didn't say anything for a while. Then he said, quietly, in Spanish, 'I want one too. For my 70th. You have four years.' He patted me on the shoulder and went to find my mom. I am, of course, going to make him one. I have no choice. I have been commissioned by my own father. I think I might use mahogany this time. Or maybe sapele. I have four years to decide. I want to start sourcing the wood by next summer.

 Camila and I lay in bed afterward not sleeping for a long time. She had her hand on my chest and we just listened to the apartment settling — the radiator, the water in the upstairs neighbor's pipes, Nube's slow breathing on the rug. She said quietly, in Portuguese because she does that when she's emotional, 'meu pai não chora.' My father doesn't cry. She said it like a small wonder. Like a fact she had grown up knowing, repeated and confirmed across thirty-three years, and which had been falsified once, tonight, by a piece of cedar I made with my hands. She said it again — 'meu pai não chora' — and then she said 'meu pai chorou hoje, e foi por você.' My father cried tonight, and it was because of you. I did not know how to receive that sentence. I still don't. I think it is going to take me a long time to know how to receive it. I just held her hand and we lay there. Eventually she fell asleep with her head on my chest and I was awake for another two hours, just thinking about the chess board on the dining table downstairs, and the brass plaque underneath, and the man in the guest room who is going to take that board home to São Paulo and play on it for years and one day give it to a grandchild who does not yet exist. The chess board is now part of a multi-generational chain. The chain extends backward through Hugo and Beatriz and Hugo's father who taught him to play chess as a child in Bahia, and forward through the unborn grandchild and the unborn great-grandchild and however many people will inherit the cedar. I made an object that is now in a chain. I cannot stop thinking about that. I think I am going to keep making objects that go into chains for the rest of my life. I am going to make my dad's board. I'm going to make Pablo's cutting board. Maybe a cradle one day. Maybe a desk for Camila. Maybe a shelf for Mateo and Sofía's first apartment. The chain is the work. The chain is the actual project of the next 40 years. The chess board for Hugo was the first link. There will be more. There have to be. Going to sleep now. The apartment is quiet. Hugo is asleep. Camila is asleep. Nube is asleep. Tomorrow is Sunday. Sunday is for cleaning.  The brass plaque thing keeps coming back. The 280 pesos of engraving is what made Hugo flip the board over. Without the plaque he might not have flipped it. Without flipping it he might not have seen the year on it, which is when his face did the second wave of the soft cry. The plaque was the second beat of the gift. I had not designed it as a second beat. It just happened. Some objects you make have second beats you don't plan for. The board taught me this. I am going to keep this lesson. Plaques on the underside of things you make are cheap and they create second beats.

 I want to record one specific thing: when Hugo set the knight on the cedar and the magnet clicked, my dad — who I know plays Italian-opening chess too — leaned forward and watched the knight settle. He was twelve again, watching his own dad set up a board. Generations of men setting up the same opening on different wood. The continuity is not romantic. The continuity is just the actual mechanics of how knowledge passes. The Italian opening lives in old men. Old men teach young men. The young men become old men. The opening lives. I am part of this chain now in some small accidental way because I made the wood that hosts the opening. The opening will outlive me on this board. Hugo's grandson — when he exists — will play the Italian on this board. The opening propagates.

Sunday morning. Apartment looks like a small bomb went off. I have been awake since 8 and I have already taken out three bags of trash, swept the living room twice, put away the chairs we borrowed from the upstairs neighbor, and wiped the dining table down four times because there was beeswax residue from the chess board and it kept showing up. Camila is still asleep. Hugo is up, sitting on the balcony with a coffee, just looking at the rooftops. We exchanged about four words this morning and they were all in Portuguese and they were all about coffee. He's leaving Tuesday. My parents flew back to Guadalajara at 7am, my mom called me from the airport to tell me she'd left half of her pan dulce in our fridge and I have to eat it before it goes stale. Mateo and Sofía are still here, sleeping on the pull-out. I have not yet thought about work tomorrow. I refuse to think about work tomorrow. It is Sunday and there is pan dulce and my father-in-law is on the balcony in the sun. He is wearing the dark blue shirt again. I do not know if he packed any other shirts. I am not going to ask. The chess board is on the dining table where Hugo set it up last night with the Italian opening. He has not moved it. He has played a few quiet moves by himself this morning. I am leaving him alone with it. The apartment smells like coffee and old mezcal and faintly of beeswax. Mateo wandered out of the guest room at 11 looking like he'd been hit by a bus and asked for water in a voice that was not his usual voice. Sofía emerged shortly after looking fine, which is correct because she had two glasses of wine all night and was clearly the only adult in the room.

 The chess board still has the Italian opening set up. Hugo played the white side. He didn't move all morning, just sat with it, occasionally moving a piece a square forward and then moving it back. He's been Italian-opening since the 1970s, since he was a young engineer in São Paulo doing surveying jobs on the weekends and playing chess with old men at a café near his apartment. He told me this once. The opening is part of his identity in a way I had not understood until tonight. Mateo emerged from the guest room at noon looking like he might survive. Sofía was already at the kitchen counter eating my mom's leftover pan dulce and asking Camila about a renovation project she's working on. The apartment slowly filled with the gentle noise of brunch. I made a fresh pot of coffee, which Hugo accepted in a small ceramic cup. He nodded at the coffee. He nodded at me. Then he turned back to the chess board. He moved a knight one square forward and then moved it back. I went out to walk Nube and gave him another hour with the board. The board is doing what I had hoped the board would do, which is to absorb him into a thing he loves and let him sit with it.  Doña Marta sent up a basket of pan dulce around 10am with a note in her precise script that said 'feliz fin de cumpleaños.' She had heard the singing from downstairs last night. I went downstairs to thank her and she waved me off and said it was nothing. Hugo had a piece with his coffee and his face did a small soft thing.

fell asleep at 9pm on the couch. just woke up confused. it's 11. going to actual bed now. maybe i'll sleep eight hours for once. nube is on the rug at my feet looking up at me with this 'are we doing this or are we doing this' face. we are doing this. moving to the bed.

 Camila is already asleep. The lamp on her side is on but she's out, book splayed across her chest. I'm going to slide it off her, mark her page with the receipt I have in my pocket from the carnicería this afternoon, turn off the lamp, and crawl in. The half-sleep on the couch was that productive kind of weird where you wake up and don't quite know what year it is. I had a flash of being 22 in my dorm at ITESM with the same crick in my neck. The body remembers what the bed feels like and what the couch feels like and tonight the body is voting for the bed. Going.  Lights off. Phone in the kitchen. Body on the bed. Brain, please. Cooperate. Just for tonight.

Back at it — opened the MIT app file again tonight. Draft 5 of the personal statement, picking it apart line by line. The opening hook ('the body is a black box from the inside') is doing a lot of work and I'm not sure it can carry it. Trying out a draft 6 opening that starts with the specific sensorimotor moment — closing my eyes during a kick combination at Atlas Box and trying to feel my hip angle without seeing the mirror, failing, and realizing that's the whole problem of motor learning. Then I pivot to the abstraction. We'll see if it lands or if it's overwrought. I think any opening that tries to be clever has a 50% chance of being insufferable. Going to read it tomorrow morning fresh and decide. Camila's read so many drafts at this point that she's started speaking in MIT-application register — last night she said 'the structural integrity of my drafting' when she was talking about the bookshelf rearrangement. We are too deep in this. The application is becoming a household idiom. Pablo offered to read draft 6 in the morning during his commute, which I have accepted, even though Pablo's commute critique is famously brutal. He once told me a presentation slide was 'doing a thing I can't tolerate' without specifying what the thing was, and made me redo the slide before he'd elaborate. I have asked him for full sentences this time. He laughed at me. He will not give me full sentences. He will give me three-word reactions and I will have to triangulate from those. This is fine. This is how he reads. Camila is doing the laptop pass tomorrow night. I'll bring printed copies to Cardinal Wednesday morning to do the printed-paper read. By Thursday I should have draft 7 locked. Friday I submit. That is the plan. We will see if the plan survives Thursday.

 Pablo is going to read draft 6 in the morning during his commute. I have explicit instructions to not text him until 11am because he says my texts ruin his commute reading. He will text back with three-word reactions and possibly an emoji and I will spend the rest of the day reverse-engineering his three words into a critique. This is the agreed protocol. Camila will do the laptop pass tomorrow night after dinner. She has explicit instructions to not be nice. I have made her a small whiteboard sign that says 'be brutal' and she has agreed to look at it before each draft. I will bring printed copies of draft 6 to Cardinal Wednesday morning to do the printed-paper read because I know the printed-paper read catches different things than the screen read. By Thursday I should have draft 7 locked, which leaves Friday for the trim pass and the submission. I have explicitly told Camila to confiscate my laptop on Saturday morning if I have not submitted by Friday at 8pm. She has agreed. We are putting this in writing. The plan is beautiful. The plan will not survive Thursday. Plans never do. The plan exists to be pivoted from. Whatever I submit on Friday will be the result of the pivot from the plan, not the execution of the plan, and that's fine.  Going to write the trim-pass diff log tomorrow morning. The diff log is a small text file where I track exactly which words I cut and why. It started as a habit at Brújula for design docs. It has migrated to the personal statement now. Diff logs are real. They make the process visible. They make the cuts feel earned.

Camila and I had this slow conversation last night before bed about her dad. She said the party was the first time since Beatriz died that she'd seen him fully present in a room — not behind a polite mask. She thinks the chess board cracked something open. She's been worried about him for a year. He lives alone in São Paulo, his social circle has shrunk because most of their friends were Beatriz's friends, and the monthly visits have been keeping him afloat but not, like, restoring him. She said something that's been sitting in my head: 'I think he was waiting for permission to feel like himself again.' Then she said, very quietly, 'and I think he just got it.' We didn't talk about kids. We didn't talk about São Paulo as a place we might one day move to. But it was in the room. We just held hands. Then she fell asleep with her hand on my chest and I lay there for another twenty minutes thinking about Hugo on the balcony in the morning. Camila brought up Dr. Muñoz and the paused therapy as we were drifting off — said maybe we book a single check-in soon, before any MIT news comes in. She wasn't anxious about it. It was a calm suggestion. I said yes. The version of me from a year ago would've stiffened at the suggestion. I noticed I didn't. I noticed I was glad she'd suggested it. Small piece of growth, real-time observable. Then we slept.

 The other thing I want to log is that Camila said tonight, almost in passing, that she'd noticed a shift in me over the last 6 weeks — said I've been 'softer' than she's seen me in two years. She said it without making it a thing. I asked what she meant. She said: 'you used to come home from work and your face was a fortress for the first 20 minutes. Now your face is just a face when you walk in.' I had not noticed this. Camila notices. The chess board work, the personal statement, even the sleep-deprived 3am rants — apparently they have, paradoxically, made me more available rather than less. The thing I'd been worried about was that the application stress would consume the relationship's bandwidth. It seems to have done the opposite. Working on something that is mine has freed the version of me that comes home, instead of dragging it down. I am going to remember this if MIT says no in February — that the application itself was a form of relationship-care, not a tax on it.  Sleeping with her hand on my chest is one of the small things I would have to learn to live without if I lost this — the relationship is built out of these small things. None of them are individually important. All of them together are the entire structure. I am very aware of this tonight.

OK Daniela emailed me asking for a one-page brief she can use as scaffolding for the rec letter. She wants bullets — projects, scope, impact — and she'll write the actual letter from there. I sat down to draft it tonight and ended up writing 1500 words because of course I did. Pasting the version I'm going to send her, slightly trimmed. Tell me if any of this reads weird before I hit send. I want it to be useful but not insufferable.

---

Daniela — here's the brief you asked for. Take whatever's useful, ignore the rest.

Professional context. I joined Brújula Logistics in late 2023 as a Mid-level backend engineer, after seven years split between Kueski (fintech, Mexican consumer credit, 2014-2017) and Wizeline (consultancy, USA banking projects, 2017-2021). Educated at ITESM Guadalajara, BSc CS, 2014. Camila kept reminding me that you'll need the dates so I'm padding them in.

Work at Brújula under your management.

1. Promotion to Senior, month 2 of arc. You put me up for the cycle right after I came back from São Paulo. The promo letter cited my work on the routing-history service redesign and the shipper-onboarding flow, but I want to make sure the rec letter centers what came after, because that's the harder evidence.

2. RouteOpt rust migration — month 6 to month 16, tech-lead. This is the big one. Background: RouteOpt was the Python/Django service that did our last-mile routing optimization. P95 latency was 340ms, which under burst conditions cascaded into queue backups and customer-facing late-pickup events. I was on the team that wrote the post-mortem for the 4-hour Postgres outage in month 5 — that doc is still on the wiki, you signed off on it. The post-mortem made the case that we'd outgrown the Python stack for that critical path. You gave me the tech-lead nod for the rust rewrite. Stack chosen: Rust, Axum, Tokio, sqlx with the PostGIS extension, Redis for caching.

Key blockers worth flagging in a letter:
- tokio-postgres didn't natively handle the PostGIS geography type. I wrote a custom encoder, open-sourced as a small crate (about 600 lines). Adoption: us, plus three external repos that have starred and forked it.
- Memory regression in months 10-11 — task-spawning leak. Tracked it down to a misuse of Tokio's spawn pattern in our request handler. Fix was a 12-line diff but the diagnosis took two weeks.
- Tokio runtime starvation under burst load, found in load testing month 14-15. Fix: bumped to multi-threaded runtime, retuned worker counts, cut p99 spike by 80%.

Result at month 16: shipped to staging. Metric: 340ms p95 → 38ms p95. ~9x improvement on the hot path. Production rollout pending now.

3. Team. I led a team of three: me, Andrés Ruiz (junior, joined month 9 — I onboarded him during the migration, which was harder than the migration itself in some ways), Carla Vela (senior, on parental leave months 11-15, which meant the back half of the migration was effectively two engineers).

Things that would not show up on a CV but might be worth a sentence:
- The migration shipped on time despite Carla's leave gap, my own back injury (lower back, sparring, month 10), my mom's medical scare (also month 10 — a week off in Guadalajara), and the manager transition when you left in month 8 and Roberto came in. That last one was a leadership tax I don't think anyone factored in.
- I wrote (or co-wrote) all the technical docs for the migration, including the post-mortem, the rust-vs-go RFC, the PostGIS encoder design doc, and the rollout runbook. I will happily share any of these if MIT wants writing samples.
- I onboarded Andrés from zero rust knowledge. He shipped his first PR to the migration in month 11 and was independently driving features by month 14. The onboarding curriculum I wrote is reusable. Carla used it when she came back from leave in month 15.

Adjacent contributions during the same window:
- Co-led the on-call playbook rewrite for the Logistics Platform team, after the postgres outage. Six runbooks, each ~3 pages.
- Mentored two interns (summer of arc month 5 and month 9 — both Mexican CS students from ITESM and UNAM). Both got return offers.
- Spoke at one internal Brújula tech talk on rust adoption decisions. The recording is on the company wiki.

Why I want the masters. (You can ignore this part for the letter, but in case you want shape — I want to study ML for nonverbal feedback in physical training, with Rosalind Picard's group at the Media Lab. I have a side project — pose-estimation for kickboxing form, paused now but real — that's the seed. The Brújula work is what I can do. The Media Lab work is what I want to learn to do.)

References for facts. Anything you want to verify with Roberto or with Carla, please feel free. Andrés will sing my praises in a way that won't be useful for you because he thinks I'm great. Carla will give you a more measured read.

Thank you, Dani. Last thing — Camila says hi, Nube says hi, and the apartment misses you. We need to get coffee before you fly out for the new gig. There's a place called Cardinal in Roma Norte I want to introduce you to.

— Seb

That's the brief. I'm going to read it twice tomorrow morning then send. Tell me if anything reads off. I'm worried it's too long. I'm worried it's too informal. Counterargument: those are genuine load-bearing facts that show I delivered the migration despite real life happening, and Daniela knows about both of them anyway because we talked about both of them in our last 1:1 before she left. So I'll leave them in. She can cut what she doesn't want.

The other version of the brief I considered — the academic-prestige version where I lead with the open-source crate and the GitHub stars and try to dress up Brújula as a hard tech company — I scrapped because Daniela can sniff that kind of thing out at 200 paces and she'd just rewrite the rec letter to undo my framing. Better to give her the messy human version with the actual texture and let her decide what's load-bearing for an admissions audience. Daniela's going to translate Mexican-startup engineer into MIT-admissions readable better than I can. I should trust her instincts.

The other thing I added at the last minute was the bit about onboarding Andrés. I went back and forth on whether 'onboarded the junior engineer' is a lever-y management story or a flex. The decision logic was: Daniela has historically valued mentorship as a signal more than IC accomplishment. She kept telling me in 1:1s that 'the test of a senior engineer is the third year, not the first year, when you're being graded on whether your team's juniors can fly without you.' That sentence has lived in my head for two years. It's the reason the Andrés bullet is in the brief. I hope she reads it and recognizes her own framework coming back at her. I think she'll smile.

 Reading the brief back tonight I noticed something I had not consciously written but which reads, to me, as load-bearing. The whole brief is structured as evidence of consistency under conditions that would have justified inconsistency. The migration shipped despite the manager change. The migration shipped despite Carla's leave. The migration shipped despite my back injury. The migration shipped despite my mom's medical scare. The migration shipped despite the postgres outage retrospective work. I had not noticed I was building a 'guy who delivers under load' frame, but reading it back, that's the frame. It's also accurate. The arc has been a long stress test of whether I can deliver while the world tries to break me. I have, mostly. The brief is an honest record. The other thing I noticed is the closing paragraph about why I want the masters — the bit about Picard's group and the pose-est project — reads as a quiet pivot from 'I delivered for Brújula' to 'I want to learn to deliver something different.' Daniela will catch this. The admissions reader might not, but Daniela will. She is going to translate it for them. That's why she's the heaviest hitter on the rec list. Translation work between Mexican Series B engineering and MIT admissions speak is the actual labor of writing a rec letter, and Daniela is uniquely positioned to do it because she has sat on both sides — she got her own masters in the US a decade ago, and she's now back in Mexico running engineering teams. She is the right human. I am sending the brief tonight after one more read. I will then send a thank-you bottle of mezcal to her new office, which she will pretend to be annoyed about and then put on her desk where everyone can see it. Daniela has a thing for Convite Pechuga. I know where to get it. I will not tell her in advance.  Camila just walked past and read over my shoulder and said the brief reads like me, which is what I wanted. Honest, slightly informal, with the structural bones of a serious document. She said the closing paragraph about Picard makes the whole thing feel like a real career narrative rather than a CV summary. Daniela will edit ruthlessly anyway. That's the point. I write loose. She tightens. The collaboration is intentional. We have done this dance before — the post-mortem doc was edited 14 times and the final version was 60% her phrasing, 40% mine. Same model. The output will be better than what I would write alone. That's why I asked her.

 One last note before I send. The brief is going to read more honest than what most senior engineers would send their former managers when asking for an MIT rec. That's intentional. Daniela has zero patience for the polished CV register. If I send her a polished CV she will write a polished generic letter, and the polished generic letter will lose to specific letters from other applicants. The unpolished honest brief gives her the raw material to write a specific letter. The specific letter beats the generic letter. The honesty is the wedge. I am sending the wedge tonight.

Draft 6 of the personal statement done. New opening landed better than I expected. The kick-with-eyes-closed paragraph works, then I move into the broader question, then one paragraph on the pose-est project as evidence of follow-through, then the research questions, then a one-paragraph close on why MIT specifically and why now.  The other thing I'm thinking about with the personal statement is the small risk of it being too cute. It's 3:14am. Doña Marta won't start the oven for another hour and forty minutes. Camila is asleep. Nube is asleep.
