The question rattling around. Am I a researcher trapped in a senior IC body, or am I a senior IC who has romanticized research because his current job is run by a politically maneuvering manager and his real talent is just being good at writing CRUD APIs really fast?

Let me list the evidence on each side. Researcher in IC body:
- I have been writing — actually writing, not just shipping — for three years. The Mastodon threads, the post-mortems, the design docs. People reach out about them. The PostGIS crate has 87 stars and a tiny community of users I correspond with.
- I read papers. Not enough, but I read them. I read the Picard 2024 paper four times. I read the MediaPipe original paper three times. I have started reading Bertinetto et al. on whatever-the-hell-it-was on a flight last month, which is not a thing senior backend engineers normally do voluntarily.
- The pose-est side project. I built it from a research question, not from a feature request. I tested hypotheses, ran sessions, plotted graphs. That's research-shaped behavior even if no academic touched it.
- I get bored quickly when problems are solved. Once RouteOpt's rust migration hit 38ms p95 I felt an immediate hollow. Researchers get hollow when problems are solved. ICs celebrate.
- I correspond with at least four academics on email — none about Brújula work, all about side topics. That's not normal IC behavior.
- The reading I do for fun overlaps with the reading I would do as a researcher. Borges, Chiang, Picard, the occasional cog-sci paper. The reading I do for industry — staff-engineer leadership books, distributed systems blog posts — feels like work even when I'm doing it on a Sunday.

IC who romanticized research:
- I'm 33. The youngest first-year MIT PhD in my cohort would be 24. (Ok masters not PhD, masters cohort more varied, but still.) I'm betting against compounding ICs and 35-year-old senior staff comp.
- I've never published. Not even a workshop paper. I have zero academic CV. Not zero — but zero peer-reviewed.
- I am addicted to the legibility of shipping. Every two weeks at Brújula I get to point at something and say 'I made this faster' and it lands in prod. Research is the opposite of legible. The dopamine cycle is months not days.
- The MIT thing might be partly about Roberto. The political-management thing has been getting under my skin since month 8. Am I trying to escape a manager I dislike, dressed up as a career pivot?
- I almost took the Wizeline job in 2017 for the money over a smaller research-y opportunity at a Mexican AI lab. I do this. I have a track record of choosing legibility over depth. Why is this time different?
- The financial calculation, sober: 95k USD eq trajectory in industry, vs. masters then either back to industry at maybe a wash, or PhD which is a 5-7 year delay against compounding. The expected-value math, narrowly defined, favors industry.
- I have not actually worked in a research environment. I have read about research environments. There is a real chance my mental model of research is romanticized.

Counter to the counter. Is it actually different? Yes — Camila has supported it openly which she would not have done if I were doing it for the wrong reasons (she has a sharp ear for that), Daniela said yes to the rec letter and Daniela is allergic to bullshit, Pablo said this morning when I asked for the wildcard letter that he'd 'been waiting for me to do this for years' which I didn't expect at all, my actual research question — nonverbal feedback in physical training — is a thing I have a verifiable kink for, and the project I keep coming back to in unstructured time is not the rust migration, it's the pose-est code.

Also: the financial expected-value math is an illusion of precision. Nobody knows what 'industry compensation' looks like in a 5-year window. Nobody knows what the academic-to-frontier-lab pipeline pays in a 5-year window. The math feels rigorous but it's stacking guesses. The qualitative question — what do you want to do with your hours — is the actually rigorous one because the answer is observable.

Another counter. The Wizeline-was-for-money pattern-match isn't quite right. Wizeline was a 22-year-old's choice with limited information, made under the gravitational field of a parent who'd just had a near-death experience. The MIT pivot is a 33-year-old's choice with full information and a 5-year track record of building things outside of work. The pattern-match is shallow. The deep pattern is different — the Wizeline-Seb wanted external validation, the Brújula-MIT-Seb wants internal alignment. Different driver. Different decision.

I think — and I'm writing this so I can read it back to myself when I doubt — I think the answer is: maybe both things are true. Maybe I am an IC who romanticized research AND a researcher trapped in IC clothing, and the way to find out is to apply, hope to get in, do the masters, and find out which one breaks first. That's the test. The test is the doing. There is no thought experiment that resolves it. There is only the doing.

The other thing — and this is the one that always quiets the spiral — is Mama Lupita. I did not visit her in her last year. She died in 2019 in Guadalajara and I had not been home in eight months because I was 'building career capital' at Wizeline, which translates to: I was working on a USA bank project that I do not now remember the name of. I traded eight months of my grandmother's last year for a project I cannot name. The exchange was not worth it. The exchange will never be worth it. Every time I doubt the MIT pivot I think about the Wizeline project I cannot name and I remember that the legible career path has a way of becoming a thing you trade your grandmother for. I would rather pivot at 33 than trade another grandmother. There are no more grandmothers to trade. I have one grandfather left. I see him at Christmas. I am going more often this year.

It's 3:42 now. I'm going to lie back down. I might be up at 4 with the bread oven. Whatever. Tomorrow I either trim the personal statement to 1000 words or I cry. Probably both. The melatonin didn't take the edge off tonight — sometimes it works, sometimes it doesn't. The CBT-I app I tried for a week in month 15 is sitting unopened on my home screen and I keep meaning to delete it because the icon stares at me accusingly. Maybe I delete it tomorrow. Maybe I open it again. Inconsistency is its own kind of insomnia. Going to bed.

 One last thing before I lie back down. The thing about Roberto. The micromanagement. The political maneuvering. The fact that he has been quietly trying to take credit for the rust migration in standups even though he didn't write a line of code or sit through a single design review. The way he has been pinging me on Slack at 9pm asking 'quick question' and then unloading 800-word strategy memos into the DM. The way he has been scheduling 1:1s and then turning them into status checks. I have been managing him for ten months. I am tired. The MIT application is partly an answer to Roberto. Partly. I want to be honest about this. The version of me that is being honest at 3am admits that one of the variables in the 'should I apply to grad school' decision was 'do I want to keep working under this manager.' The answer was no. The application is partly a reaction to that no. But the application is also more than that. The application is the positive reason — Picard, the research questions, the pose-est project. The Roberto reason is the negative reason. Both are true. I am not going to pretend the negative reason isn't there. I am also not going to let it be the dominant reason. If MIT says no, I will not be re-stuck under Roberto for the next 5 years — I will leave Brújula for a different role somewhere else, regardless. The Roberto-shaped problem is independent of the MIT outcome. I should solve it independently. The MIT path is the positive path. The 'leave Roberto' path is the parallel obligation that exists regardless. I am writing this down so 3am-Sebastián stops conflating them. They are separate questions with separate answers. OK. Going to lie back down. Doña Marta starts the oven in 32 minutes. I might smell the bread before I sleep. That is OK.  One more 3am rumination before I shut up. The thing I keep noticing is that my body is not in revolt. I am sleep-deprived but I am not unwell. The lower back is at 70 percent. The kickboxing is going. The yerba mate works. The cardio is fine. The body is holding the application like a small load that the body can carry. The mind is what is generating the noise. The mind always generates the noise. The body is the steadier instrument. I should listen to the body more than the mind. The body says: this is fine. The mind says: imagine these 47 catastrophes. The body wins. Going to lie back down. Body wins.

 Last absolute last thing. Mama Lupita's bombilla on the kitchen counter. I am going to use it tomorrow morning. Eight years since she gave it to me. Eight years of mornings. The cup has held maybe 3000 cups of mate. The metal at the rim is darker now than it was when she handed it to me. The darkening is the record of every morning I sat with my own thinking. The cup keeps the count. I am going to make her proud tomorrow. I am going to make her proud the next day too. I am going to keep going.

Pre-submission jitters in full force. The application is sitting in the MIT portal in 'ready to submit' state. Personal statement is at draft 7 and locked at 994 words. Recs are queued — Daniela's letter went in this afternoon, Pablo says his is a few days out, Lozano is the wild card timing-wise but she said she'd send by Wednesday. I keep refreshing the portal even though nothing changes when I refresh it. Camila told me to step away from the laptop. I am not stepping away from the laptop. Tomorrow I submit. Probably. Maybe. We'll see how I feel tomorrow morning. I might submit tonight. I might submit at 2am. I might wait until Friday. Who knows. Brain is mush. The cursor is hovering over the submit button. I have hovered over it for approximately three hours of cumulative time today. Camila has made dinner without me twice this week. I owe her at least one nice dinner where I am physically and mentally present. Realized at 11pm tonight that I should also write a brief acknowledgments-and-thanks email to Daniela, Pablo, and Lozano regardless of outcome. The submission of an MIT application is a not-quite-public event in their lives too — they've put their reputation behind my name. I should mark it. I drafted three versions of the email.  I keep thinking about the people who'd be reading the application packet in February or March. The admissions readers at MIT Media Lab. Picard's group reviewing fit. The faculty making cohort decisions. Strangers in Cambridge whose hands my future runs through. I have an irrational urge to thank them too — to send a one-line note via the portal that just says 'thank you for reading.' I will not do this. It would read as ingratiating and it would not help. But the urge is real and it's interesting that the urge is there. The application is a one-way communication. They evaluate. I wait. The asymmetry of attention is part of what makes the wait so hard. They have my whole written self in front of them. I have nothing of theirs. I will learn their decision through a one-line email in February. That's the relationship. I find it strangely intimate and strangely cold simultaneously.  I keep thinking about how each rec letter is now a small artifact of the relationship that produced it. Daniela's letter is shaped like five years of management. Lozano's letter is shaped like 12 years of low-frequency academic correspondence. Pablo's letter is shaped like 6 years of friendship and 12 sparring sessions. The packet is, accidentally, an autobiography in three voices.

submitted. green checkmark. confirmation email landed. hands shaking slightly. mate going cold next to me. nube on my foot. camila in the kitchen. 6:47pm october 24 2026. it's done. it's in mit's hands. i am going to walk into the kitchen and tell her now.

 The portal page now says 'application complete — under review.' That's it. The button is gone. The thing I have been wrestling with for six weeks is now a record in a database somewhere in Cambridge. Camila is going to come out of the kitchen any second and see my face and she will know. I want to remember the texture of this exact 30 seconds. The hum of the refrigerator. Nube on my foot. The mate gone cold. The portal screen still glowing. Going. Going to her now.  Walking to the kitchen now. She is humming something. Going.

OK so what just happened. I refreshed the page one more time. The 'Submit Application' button was glowing at me. I closed my eyes — like literally closed my eyes — and clicked it. Got the green checkmark. Got the email confirmation. Walked into the kitchen where Camila was making tea and said 'I did it.' She put the kettle down and hugged me without saying anything for a long time. Now I'm sitting on the couch and my hands are kind of shaking and I don't know if it's adrenaline or the four mates I had today. Probably both. Nube can sense something is up and is sitting on my foot looking concerned. I keep checking my email like another confirmation might come through. It will not. The application is now in a queue with thousands of other applications. The decision drops in February. The next 90 days are going to be an exercise in not refreshing the portal. I am going to fail at this exercise. I texted the three people who'd carried me through this — Pablo, Daniela, Lozano — and said 'submitted, thank you for everything.' Pablo replied with three exclamation points. Daniela replied 'orgullosa.' Lozano replied with a paragraph in formal Spanish that I am going to have to read three times to take in. Camila and I are going to Nudo for celebration ramen. I am putting the phone in airplane mode for two hours. The portal cannot tempt me from inside an airplane mode.

 I keep thinking about how it felt physically to click the button. The click itself was nothing — it was a button, it was a normal button, the kind I click 1000 times a day. But my finger took maybe 90 seconds to land on it. I positioned the cursor. I held the mouse. I closed my eyes. I clicked. The click sounded like every other click. The page reloaded. The green checkmark appeared. The email landed in my inbox 14 seconds later. The whole thing took maybe 30 seconds end to end and the entirety of it is now unforgettable. Camila has just come back into the living room with two cups of chamomile and is making me put my phone away. She is right. The phone goes in the other room. The application is gone. The next 90 days are mine.  Camila and I are going to walk to Nudo and back. The cold air is going to feel good on my face. I am going to put my phone in my coat pocket and not check it. The application is in. The waiting starts tomorrow. Tonight is for ramen.

MIT portal updated — Daniela's recommendation letter has been received. Got the little 'Letter received' tag next to her name in the dashboard. She emailed me separately to say 'sent. you got this. don't refresh the portal.' I am of course going to refresh the portal. The fact that her letter is in is genuinely the biggest weight off my chest because she was the heaviest hitter on my list — former eng manager, has actual context on RouteOpt and the rust migration metrics. I asked her if she'd share the letter with me afterward and she said maybe in five years when I either am or am not a Picard student. Fair. She also said her new gig is going well, that she's already two months in and feels like she made the right call leaving Brújula. Then she said 'tell Roberto I said hi' which is a joke, I think, because I am pretty sure Daniela does not like Roberto and Roberto definitely does not like Daniela. I am not going to tell Roberto anything. Things stay where they belong. She added at the bottom of the email that she'd written one paragraph in the rec letter specifically about how I'd handled the manager transition between her and Roberto — said it was 'one of the things she'd most respected' from her last six months at Brújula. I had not realized she'd noticed. Apparently she had noticed. Apparently she'd told her new boss about it. Mortifying and gratifying at the same time. The rec letter is going to land in front of an admissions reader who has no context on Mexican Series B startup politics, and is going to read 'managed transition' in a generic way, but the thing Daniela was actually praising is real and I'm going to take the praise even if no one else hears it the way she meant it.

 I want to log something specific about Daniela's letter that I'm trying not to dwell on too much but is sitting with me. She mentioned in her email that she had asked Carla, behind my back, for a 'sanity check' on whether the paragraph she'd written about me handling the manager transition was accurate. Carla had apparently said 'oh that's understated, the actual story is worse than that' and Daniela had taken note. I had no idea this was happening. Daniela was triangulating with my current colleague behind my back to make sure her rec letter was honest. That level of care from a former manager — six months out from working together, with no incentive to do extra work — is something I do not want to forget. I will reciprocate when she eventually wants something from me. Could be in five years, could be in ten. The favor bank with Daniela is one I want to keep deep.  I keep wondering whether Daniela's letter mentions the Mastodon thread on Tokio runtime starvation. She read it when I posted it and complimented me on the technical writing in our last 1:1. The letter probably references it. I will never know. The letter is sealed.

And Lozano's letter is in. Dr. Patricia Lozano, my ITESM thesis advisor from 2014. She emailed me a heads-up before submitting. Twelve years since I sat in her office. She said in the email that she'd had to think hard about what to write because I'd been a 'good student but not the most academically ambitious one she'd advised that year.' (Brutal. Accurate.) She said the letter focuses on intellectual curiosity sustained over a decade — the fact that I've kept emailing her once or twice a year about random research papers and asking her opinion. I had not realized I'd been doing that. Apparently I have been doing that. She thinks the consistency itself is the evidence. Honestly I love that framing. Three rec letters in. Just Pablo's left. She also said — and I'm going to quote because it's making me a little emotional — 'you have always been a question-asker. The masters is the right venue for question-askers.' I am not crying I am just sitting with that for a while. She also asked, almost in passing, whether I'd be open to an interview or a follow-up call with anyone at MIT who wanted to triangulate on her letter. I said yes obviously. Twelve years of academic distance just collapsed into one email. She closed with 'estoy contenta de saber que sigues siendo curioso, Sebastián. Cuídate.' I had to read that line about four times before I could move on with my evening.

 I want to remember something about the email exchange with Lozano. We've kept in light touch since 2014 — once or twice a year, like she said — but I had not realized she was reading the random papers I was forwarding her with attention. She said something in the email like 'your three notes about the 2023 Bertinetto paper actually changed how I'm framing my own seminar this semester.' I had no idea. I had thought I was just shooting random papers into a void out of vague intellectual loneliness. Apparently the void was reading. She also said she'd been waiting for me to either write something serious or apply somewhere serious, and was glad it was the second. The 12-year academic distance was not as distant as I'd thought. There's a small lesson in here about long, low-frequency, low-pressure correspondence with people who matter. The slow drip is its own form of relationship maintenance. I will keep emailing her papers. I will probably do it more often.  I am going to email Lozano back tomorrow and ask if she'd be willing to meet for coffee in Guadalajara when I'm up at Christmas. We have not been in the same room since 2014. I want to close the distance. She will say yes. I am pretty sure of it.

the waiting is going to be its own kind of torture, isn't it. four months until the decision. a hundred and twenty mornings of opening the portal and seeing 'under review' and closing it again. four months of pretending to be normal at brújula stand-ups. four months. i can feel the insomnia warming up already. The decision drops in February — they said early February, the form letter said 'we typically notify by mid-February' — so call it February 12 plus or minus a week. That's about 110 days from now. 110 mornings of opening the portal. 110 nights of going to sleep with the portal closed. The MIT decision dropdown is going to become a daily ritual whether I want it to or not. Camila has already said she's going to put a child-lock on the laptop, which would be funny except she's somewhat serious. I have agreed to refresh the portal at most twice a day. Morning with mate, evening before bed. That is the rule. We will see if I can keep it. Honestly, knowing my brain,

Camila asked me tonight what the personal statement journey was actually like — not the content, the process. I told her: seven drafts. SEVEN. Let me retrospect on each because I want to remember the shape of this for the next time I have to write something hard.

Draft 1. Written month 17, week 1. Two evenings, both with mezcal. 1600 words, way over. Opened with the black belt moment — Profe handing the belt, Pablo recording on his phone, the family flying in, the way Profe held my shoulders for three seconds before tying the belt. Spent 400 words on that scene. Camila read it and said 'this is a great essay but it's not for MIT.' She was right. It was for me. I'd been wanting to write the black-belt scene since the night it happened and the personal statement was just the first excuse. Saved that draft as 'belt_essay_dont_throw_away.md' and started over. I'm going to come back to that essay one day. Maybe as a Mastodon post. Maybe as something for a blog I don't have yet. The scene deserves its own home.

Draft 2. Different week. Written sober, on my ThinkPad at café Cardinal. 1100 words. Way too academic. Two paragraphs of literature review. Citations to three Picard papers. No personal voice. Sounded like a grad-school applicant who had read 'how to write a personal statement' Substacks. Pablo read this one and said 'who is this' and I knew it was over. The funny thing is I was so proud of draft 2 when I finished it. It felt like the kind of thing a serious applicant would write. That feeling is the warning signal. If a draft feels like the-kind-of-thing-someone-would-write, it's not the kind of thing you would write, and admissions readers can sniff it out.

Draft 3. Third week. Tried to compromise between 1 and 2. 1300 words. Half-personal, half-academic. The seam between the halves was visible. Camila called it 'the centaur draft.' Front legs of a horse, back legs of a person, awkward. She wasn't being cruel. She was being precise. The metaphor stuck.

Draft 4. Fourth week. Threw out drafts 1-3 and started from a single sentence I'd written at 2am: 'The body is a black box from the inside.' Built outward from there. 950 words. Tighter, but Pablo said the opening line sounded 'philosophy-grad-student-y.' He was right. I sat on it for two days. I was tempted to defend it because I liked the sentence. I had to remind myself that liking your own sentences is the second-warning signal of personal-statement writing. The good sentences in your statement should be the ones the readers like, not the ones you like. Different judges.

Draft 5. Same skeleton as 4, different opening. Tried 'When I was a blue belt I asked my coach if my hip rotation was right and he said sure, sebas, it's right' as the hook. Pablo said 'this one's better.' Camila said it was undermining itself in the second paragraph — said I was hedging on the research questions in a way that read as insecure. 1010 words. Submitted to a friend-of-a-friend who's an MIT PhD for a quick read — she said the research questions were strong but the personal scaffolding was 'overcorrecting from autobiographical to performative-humble.' That note was useful. She also said something I have not been able to forget: 'the strongest personal statements are the ones where you can hear the writer's voice arguing with themselves on the page.' I had been editing out my own arguments, trying to sound certain. The certainty was the problem.

Draft 6. Took the friend's note. Revised the personal scaffolding. Allowed the arguing-with-myself to come back in. New opening — 'I closed my eyes during a kick combination and tried to feel my hip angle. I couldn't.' Then pivoted faster into the broader question. 1023 words. Camila said 'this is the one.' I didn't trust her because she's said that about three other things this year — including a Picard paper title I tried out as a working subtitle, which she'd liked and then I'd cut anyway because Pablo had been confused by it.

Draft 7. Trim pass. Cut 29 words. Tightened two transitions. Re-read it three times in three different settings (laptop, phone, printed on paper) to catch tone issues that don't show up in just one medium. Locked at 994 words. Submitted with the application this evening. The final draft has the kick-with-eyes-closed opening, one paragraph on the pose-est project as proof of follow-through, three paragraphs on research questions about nonverbal sensorimotor feedback, one paragraph on why MIT and why Picard's group, and a final 80 words on what I'd want to do after the masters.

What I learned. The thing I'll tell anyone who asks me about this is: the first draft is for you. You have to write it before you can write the real one. I needed to get the black-belt-moment essay out of my system before I could write a personal statement that wasn't secretly about kickboxing. I'd been carrying that essay around since month 15 and it was getting in the way. Once I wrote it and shelved it, I could think about MIT clearly.

The other thing I learned. Pablo and Camila are different readers. Pablo flags voice problems — when I sound like I'm trying too hard, or when I sound like someone other than me. Camila flags structural problems — when the seams show, when the argument doesn't hang together. I needed both. If I'd had only Pablo I would've sent draft 5. If I'd had only Camila I would've sent draft 4. Together they got me to draft 7. Owe them both.

Third thing. Word counts are tyrants but they're useful tyrants. Every cut from 1300 to 950 made the draft better. Every cut from 1010 to 994 also made the draft better. The worst draft I wrote was the 1600-word one because no constraint was forcing me to choose. The best draft I wrote was the one that hit the hard ceiling exactly. Constraints sharpen.

Fourth thing. Read it in three media. Laptop reading misses tone problems that the phone catches. Phone reading misses structural problems that printed paper catches. Printed paper misses run-on sentences that the laptop catches in the line wrap. I caught two genuine errors in the printed-paper read of draft 7 that all the laptop reads had missed. I will do this for everything important from now on. I had not done this before. I will do it now.

Fifth thing — and this one I want to remember most. The seven drafts took six weeks and felt like six months. The decision to count them as iterations and not as failures was psychologically load-bearing. Every time a draft came back wrong I had a 30-minute window where my brain wanted to catastrophize and conclude I was an impostor. Camila or Pablo would say 'cool, what did you learn from that one,' and the framing of 'what did you learn' would convert the catastrophe into a data point. I would learn something. I would write the next draft. The thing that kept me writing was the framing, not the content. Carry this lesson forward into anything hard.

Sixth thing. The black-belt-essay artifact, saved as 'belt_essay_dont_throw_away.md', is the most valuable byproduct of this process. It exists because it had to. It needed to be written for me to write the real thing. I have learned that some essays are precursor essays whose only job is to free the next one. I will probably write more precursor essays in my life. I will not be embarrassed by them. They are the cost of the real essay.

What's done is done. The portal says 'submitted.' Now I wait. Camila just brought me chamomile tea because I will not stop talking about the application. I am going to drink the tea and shut up about it for one whole evening. Probably.

 One more thing about the seven-draft journey. The act of writing draft 1 — the wrong draft, the autobiographical-belt-essay draft — was paradoxically the most important act in the whole sequence. If I had not written draft 1 the way I wrote it, with its 400 words about Profe handing me the belt and Pablo recording on his phone, I would not have been able to write the disciplined draft 7. Draft 1 was the catharsis. Draft 7 was the discipline. The catharsis had to come first. The discipline could not have come first. I would have been editing a corpse instead of editing a living thing. I want to remember this for any other hard writing project I take on. Write the wrong draft first. Write it loose, write it long, write it from your gut, do not show it to anyone, save it as 'draft_1_dont_throw_away.md', and then start over. The draft 1 will live as a private artifact. The draft 7 will be the public artifact. The world will see the draft 7 and assume you wrote it directly. They will be wrong. They will not know about the draft 1. The draft 1 was the parent of the draft 7. The parent is invisible. The parent has to be invisible for the draft 7 to look professional. But the parent is real. I have file 'belt_essay_dont_throw_away.md' as evidence. I am keeping it. I will probably never publish it. I will read it in five years and remember why the personal statement exists. The personal statement exists because the belt essay was written first. Submit the personal statement. Save the belt essay. Tell the next person who has to write something hard: write the wrong draft first.  One more meta-observation about the whole drafting arc. The seven drafts took me 47 days. The first draft I have logged in git is October 1. Draft 7 was committed on October 23. Submission was October 24. So 23 days from first draft to submitted final, with the bulk of the drafts produced in week 3 and week 4. The first two weeks were structural exploration. The last two weeks were execution. The pattern is identical to how I write technical design docs at Brújula — long exploration, then a sudden week where the doc converges. I had not noticed I was using the same writing pattern across both contexts. Useful self-knowledge.

 Last meta-thought. The friend-of-a-friend MIT PhD note about 'arguing with yourself on the page' has stuck with me beyond this application. I think it generalizes. Arguments-with-yourself are the texture of any honest writing. Documents that don't argue with themselves are advertisements. The personal statement that I submitted has, I hope, two visible arguments — the autobiographical-vs-academic tension, and the rehabilitation-vs-sports-science framing. Both arguments are visible. I left them visible because I trust the reader to follow them. If the reader can't follow them I can't help them. The argument is the work.

Pablo's letter just hit the portal. Last of the three. He texted me 'hecho' and a picture of him in the metro looking smug. He also forwarded me the letter, which he should not have done, but Pablo is Pablo. I read it. It's three paragraphs. The first is about the pose-est project — about how I built it because I wanted an answer to a question, not because anyone asked me to, and how he was a test user and watched me iterate on it for seven months. The second is about — and I'm paraphrasing so I don't quote it directly — how I'm the kind of friend who reads papers on the bus and texts him about them at 1am. He calls this 'pathological intellectual generosity' which is the warmest insult I've ever received. The third is one sentence: 'Sebastián should be a researcher and you should let him.' That's it. Three paragraphs. The wildcard letter is exactly what a wildcard letter should be. I have no notes. Pablo also signed off the email he sent me with 'admissions can stop reading at the third paragraph imo' which is exactly what he'd say. All three letters in. Application complete. Now I genuinely just wait. The portal status field went from 'awaiting recommenders' to 'application complete — under review.' I screenshotted the status change. Camila will hate that I screenshotted it. I am going to delete the screenshot tomorrow morning, after I have looked at it twelve more times tonight. Maybe thirteen.

 I'm trying to decide whether to thank Pablo with something — beers don't feel like enough, but he'd refuse anything bigger. The wildcard letter from a peer is a particular kind of vulnerable on his end. He wrote three paragraphs about me to a stranger at MIT. He took time on the metro to draft it on his phone. He attached his name to my application packet. The asymmetry of debt is a thing I keep wrestling with — Daniela had a manager-shaped reason, Lozano had an academic-advisor-shaped reason, but Pablo just had a friendship-shaped reason. There's no professional capital in writing a peer rec letter. He just did it because he believes I should be doing this. I am going to make him a small cutting board for his birthday in March. Not as repayment — there is no repayment — but as a hand-made acknowledgment of the kind of thing the rec letter was. Camila has agreed this is the right move. The cutting board project starts in December as a parallel to the second chess board for my dad.

Camila and I had dinner at Nudo tonight to celebrate the submission. She had the spicy miso. I had the shio. She raised her glass and said 'to my partner the future MIT student.' I said 'don't jinx it.' She said 'it's not a jinx, it's a wish.' She told me she's been quietly proud watching this whole thing because — and she said this verbatim — 'you've been the one supporting my career moves for five years and this is the first one that's been really yours.' Her Pritzker mentor nomination, my Brújula promo, the Bogotá decline — those were collaborative life-decisions. The MIT app was mine. She said 'I wanted you to have something that was just yours.' I had to look at the menu for a while because I didn't have a response that wasn't going to be embarrassing in public. After dinner we walked back through Roma Norte and the streetlights had that warm October sodium-yellow that makes everything look like a memory in advance. We didn't talk much on the walk. We didn't need to. When we got home she went to brush her teeth and I sat on the couch with Nube and watched the apartment for a while. Hugo's chess board is still on the dining table. Hugo is back in São Paulo by now. The board has the Italian opening still on it from the night of the party. I haven't moved a piece. Camila walked past it on the way to bed and said 'we should leave it like this for a while.' I agreed. Sometimes a board mid-game is the most accurate state for an apartment to be in.

 The other thing she said tonight that's been turning over in my head: 'whatever happens with MIT, you're not the same person you were a year ago.' She said it in a particular tone — not cliche, not romantic, more like an observation a mentor would make. I asked her what she meant. She said 'you're more honest about what you want. You used to dress up wants as obligations. You stopped doing that this year.' That landed. I had not noticed I was doing the dressing-up. Apparently I was. She named it before I did. There's a kind of intimacy in being named by someone better at noticing than you are. I'd been thinking the MIT pivot was a logistics question. She'd been seeing it as an honesty question. She is, of course, right. The pivot was always about getting honest.  On the walk home she asked me, in a way that was both casual and load-bearing, whether I was happy. I said yes. She said good. She said she had been wanting to ask for weeks and had been waiting for the right moment. The walk was the right moment. The streetlights were the right light. The submission was the right pretext. Sometimes happiness is asked about correctly only after the prerequisites are in place.

walking nube. light rain. she's nervous but holding it together. trazodone helping. small win. 21 months in and we are slowly fixing the thunder thing. nube is looking up at me every fifteen seconds checking that the rain is a controlled rain and not the bad rain. controlled rain. small good moments add up.

 The streetlights are reflecting off the wet pavement and Nube keeps stopping at every reflection like it might be a portal.
