OK. People keep asking what the personal statement actually said and I'm tired of summarizing it badly. Here is the final draft, draft 7, 994 words, submitted to MIT Media Lab Affective Computing group on October 24, 2026. Pasting in full because some of you have asked. I will probably read it back in five years and cringe. But this is the version of me that submitted it.

---

I closed my eyes during a kick combination and tried to feel my hip angle. I couldn't.

My coach at Atlas Box CDMX, Esteban Domínguez, had been telling me for two years that my left hook chambered late and my hip rotation lagged on the second kick of a combination. I would nod, throw the combination again, and feel — to me — exactly the same as before. The disconnect between what my coach saw and what I felt was the most persistent technical problem in my training, and the most persistent technical problem of human motor learning more broadly. The body is a black box from the inside, especially when you're learning. The body doesn't tell you what it's doing. It tells you what it intended to do.

I am a senior backend engineer. I work at Brújula Logistics, a Series B last-mile delivery startup in Mexico City, where I lead a small team migrating our route-optimization service from Python to Rust. The work is about making large systems behave under load. I am good at it. But the question that has consumed my unstructured hours for the past 18 months is the question I just described — how to give athletes, and patients, and learners of any physical skill, an honest answer to the question 'what is my body actually doing?' that doesn't depend on a coach being in the room.

In pursuit of that question I built a side project: a pose-estimation system for kickboxing form analysis. I started with TensorFlow, found it too slow on commodity hardware, and rebuilt on top of MediaPipe Pose. I instrumented twelve sparring sessions of my own and three friends'. I plotted hip-rotation angle against kick-chamber timing. I generated frame-by-frame visualizations of the moments where motion deviated from intent. The system told me what my coach had been telling me for two years: my second-kick chamber timing was inconsistent. But unlike my coach's verbal feedback, which I had heard a thousand times and could not internalize, the visualization made the deviation legible. I could see the gap between intention and motion. Within four sessions of using my own tool, the gap had closed by an order of magnitude. I earned my black belt — first dan — at Atlas Box four months ago.

This is not a personal statement about kickboxing. The kickboxing is illustrative. The point is the broader question of nonverbal sensorimotor feedback, and the proposition that machine learning systems are uniquely positioned to close the proprioceptive gap that human teachers cannot. A coach can describe the body. They cannot show you the body to itself in real time, framed in coordinates the body's owner can act on.

The research questions I want to study at the Media Lab are these. First: what classes of motor-learning problem are best suited to ML-mediated nonverbal feedback, and what classes are not? My intuition, formed by my pose-est work, is that high-frequency, fast-feedback skills — combat sports, dance, instrumental music — benefit most. Slow, embodied skills — yoga, weightlifting form — may benefit less. I want to test this systematically. Second: what is the right modality of feedback for the body's loop? Visual? Haptic? Auditory? My pose-est work used visual feedback. Haptic feedback, delivered post-rep, may work better for sports where eye contact with the opponent is required. I want to study modality choice as a research question, not as a UX afterthought. Third: how do we evaluate this work? The ML community has good metrics for pose estimation and for emotion detection. We do not have good metrics for whether a learner is actually closing the gap between intention and motion. I want to contribute on the evaluation side.

These questions sit at the intersection of affective computing and motor learning, and I believe Rosalind Picard's group at the Media Lab is the place to study them. I have read and re-read Professor Picard's work on extending affective computing beyond emotion to broader bodily self-knowledge — the threads in her later writing that argue for affective systems as augmentations of human introspection rather than substitutes for it. That framing is exactly the framing I have been groping toward, alone, for the past 18 months. I want to study with the person who has been articulating it.

I come to this without an academic publication record. My evidence of capability is the work I have done at Brújula — shipped systems, post-mortems, technical writing — and the side project that birthed the question I want to study. The masters program is, for me, a deliberate pivot. I have nine years of industry behind me, a clear research interest, and the time and willingness to start over in a research context. I am 33. I am not the youngest applicant in my cohort. I do not believe I am the least serious.

In five years I want to be the person who has built systems that close proprioceptive gaps for athletes recovering from injury, for stroke patients relearning motion, for surgical residents shortening their training time. I want to do this in a research context first, and then in industry, where the question of whether the work meets people will be settled.

The body is a black box from the inside. I want to learn how to crack it open, gently, with tools that respect the bodies they're attached to. I would like to study at the Media Lab.

— Sebastián Río Castellanos

---

That's the thing. 994 words. I will never read it again voluntarily. It is now MIT's problem. Anyone who's asked me to summarize it in two sentences over the last week — sorry, here's all of it. The two-sentence version always failed. The full version is the only honest version. Read it or don't. Tell me if it lands. Or don't. I am bracing for opinions and also for the absence of opinions, and I am unsure which will be worse for my brain. Camila has set a rule that nobody is allowed to discuss the personal statement in the apartment after 9pm because she correctly identified that I will spiral. The rule starts tonight. It is currently 9:04pm. This post counts as me publishing the statement before the curfew kicks in. Loophole observed. The rule is now in effect. Going to put the laptop away.

 Reading it back now, four days after submission, the thing that strikes me is how compressed it is. 994 words to summarize a research interest, a project, a career pivot, an autobiographical hook, three research questions, an advisor preference, and a reason for switching domains at 33. Most of the content is implied rather than stated. The kickboxing paragraph implies five years of training. The Brújula paragraph implies a senior IC role with nontrivial deliverables. The 'I am 33' line implies the whole career-pivot conversation that I'd been having with Camila for six months. The reader has to fill in the gaps. The gaps are the work. A bad personal statement makes the reader fill in the wrong gaps. A good personal statement makes the reader fill in approximately the right gaps. I think this one does the second thing. I will not know for sure until February. The other thing I notice reading it back is that the voice is mine — I can hear myself in the sentences, the way I'd talk if I were telling Pablo about the project over beers, except cleaned up by 30%. Pablo's note in draft 5 — 'finally sounds like you' — was the right note. The voice is doing more work than the content in admissions packets. Picard reads thousands of these. The ones she remembers are the ones with voice. I think this one has voice. The risk is that the voice is too informal — that 'the body is a black box from the inside' reads as performative and not as plain. I have decided not to worry about it. The line is the line. If they don't like the voice they don't admit me and I work on the voice for the next round. The voice is not infinitely tunable. I'm not going to write in someone else's voice to win an admissions decision.  The thing I keep wanting to add to the personal statement, even retroactively now that it's submitted, is one sentence about Hugo and the chess board. Some line like 'this past month I made a chess board for my father-in-law's 70th birthday and learned how a hand-made object can carry meaning that purchased objects cannot.' But it would have been gratuitous in the personal statement. The personal statement was about ML and motor learning. The chess board was about a different question. I cut the line. The line lives here in this log. Some sentences belong to the personal statement and some belong to the journal. I am learning the difference.

 Last thought before bed. I sent the cold email to Picard at 4pm. She has not replied. She probably will not reply. The email is now sitting in her inbox where it might or might not get read. The lottery ticket is purchased. The expected return is whatever it is. The act of having purchased it is now a fact in the world that cannot be unmade. I am going to forget about it for three months. The portal is closed. Camila is asleep. Nube is on the rug. The night is quiet.

Day after submission. Slept 7 hours last night which is the most consecutive sleep I've had in months. Apparently the cortisol of having an unsubmitted application sitting in a portal was non-trivial, and removing it has measurable effects on my sleep architecture. I woke up at 7am, made yerba mate, walked Nube, and spent two hours doing literally nothing on the couch. Camila said it's the first time in three months she's seen me without a laptop on my legs. Going to give myself a week of nothing before I start thinking about the next thing. Or, well, that's the plan. We'll see if the brain cooperates. I noticed a small thing this morning — I instinctively reached for my laptop after breakfast and there was nothing on the immediate horizon to work on. The reach was a habit. The void on the other side of the reach was new. I'm trying to sit with the void instead of filling it. Lasted about 40 minutes before I opened a Picard paper anyway. Camila laughed when she walked past and saw me with the paper. She said 'sitting with the void, are we?' I said 'shut up.' She said 'OK enjoy your void.' She kissed me on the head and went to do actual work. The Picard paper is a 2019 one I hadn't read carefully — about extending stress-detection wearables into bodily-state-awareness feedback. There are two paragraphs in section 4 that read like she has been waiting for me to finish my masters and join her group. I underlined them. I will not write to her about them yet. The cold-email-to-Picard idea I had at 3am still feels overconfident. Let it sit.

 Mid-afternoon I sat down and started drafting the cold-email to Picard. Three sentences. Took me 90 minutes to land on the right three sentences. The first sentence is just 'Dear Professor Picard, I am writing to introduce myself as an applicant to your group's masters program for the 2027 cohort.' Plain. The second sentence: 'I came to your work via your 2024 paper on extending affective computing into bodily self-knowledge, which articulated a thread I had been groping toward in my own side-project research on ML-mediated proprioceptive feedback in physical training.' Specific. Third sentence: 'I am not asking for anything — I am simply noting that one of your papers was load-bearing for me, and I look forward to the possibility of contributing to your group.' Closed. Sent. The whole email is 110 words. I drafted seven versions. The version I sent is the one that doesn't ask for anything. That's the version that respects her time. Camila proofread. She approved. I clicked send before I could second-guess. Now whatever happens, the email exists. The asymmetric upside lottery ticket is purchased.  Camila brought me a bowl of soup for lunch and laughed at me for having read seven pages of the Picard paper since breakfast. She said 'so the void is full of Picard now' and walked away. The void is, in fact, full of Picard now. The void compromised. I compromised back. Mutual.

Back at Atlas Box tonight for the first time in two weeks. Profe noticed immediately. He said 'estás otra vez,' you're back, and made me do a long warmup before letting me onto the bag. Light rounds only. The lower back is at maybe 70% — PT has been getting me there slowly, the Clínica Querétaro physio is a saint — but I could throw clean combinations again without that pinching sensation. The black-belt patch on my gym bag still surprises me when I see it. It's been two months. You'd think I'd be used to it. Pablo asked if I'm planning to compete now. I said no. He said 'pendejo' affectionately. Then he hit me with a body shot. Profe pulled me aside at the end of class and asked, in his slow careful Spanish, whether the MIT thing was 'real real or just real.' I said real real. He nodded. Said 'be back to teach the white belts when you have time.' I will. The Fairtex shin guards are still holding up after two and a half years of daily use, faded red now where they used to be brick red. The Twins Special gloves smell like something between sweat and copper and there's not enough Lysol in the world to fix that. I do not want to fix that. They smell like home. I went home, took a long shower, and ate cold leftover poblano pasta straight out of the tupperware standing at the kitchen counter. Camila walked in, watched me for a second, and said 'you look happier than you've looked in three months.' I think she's right. Sparring fixed something the application had not.

 On the walk home from Atlas Box Pablo asked, almost in passing, whether I thought I'd miss the gym in Boston. I told him: yes, deeply, and that's part of the math. There's a kickboxing gym he'd already googled in Cambridge — he sent me the link last week, unsolicited — that has decent reviews and a good Mexican-Brazilian-Cuban representation. I'm going to walk in there if I get accepted. I am not going to start over from white belt though. I am going to walk in with my black-belt patch on my bag and let the gym tell me what they want to do with me. Maybe I just train. Maybe I help teach. The body knows what it knows and a different gym can take or leave that knowledge. Pablo says I should not train at the new gym for the first month — should just observe — and I think he's right. The body needs to read the room before throwing a kick.  Profe also asked me, before I left, whether I've been sleeping. I told him sometimes. He said 'sleep more.' He says this every two months. He is right every two months. I have not yet figured out the trick to sleeping more on demand. He thinks the trick is just to do it. He may be right.

tired in a clean way tonight. body tired, not brain tired. forgot what this felt like. there's a difference between the bone-tired you get after a hard workout and the static-tired you get after a long mental project, and i had been living in the second one for so long i'd forgotten what the first one was. body tired is friendly tired. Camila came in, sat next to me, looked at me, and said 'mi amor, this is your post-application body, this is the body that has put a thing down.' She rubbed my shoulder. I made one bad joke about cortisol curves. She tolerated it. We sat there for a few minutes. Nube came over and put her chin on my knee. The apartment was quiet in a different quiet than usual — not the strained quiet of a working evening, not the social quiet of a party morning, just the genuine quiet of a Tuesday with nothing left to deliver. I had forgotten that texture. I want to remember it. Tomorrow is just

OK so this has been brewing for weeks and I want to lay it out properly because I'm going to need to come back and read this when I'm doubting myself. The career-pivot question. The rumination.

The path I'm on without changing anything: Senior Backend Engineer at Brújula, currently 95k USD eq. Roberto as manager. Likely promotion to Staff in 12-18 months given how RouteOpt landed. Brújula goes Series C in 2027 maybe, equity probably worth something, maybe a lot, maybe nothing. I stay 3-5 more years. I become a Staff Engineer at a Series C/D company, comp scales to maybe 130-150k USD eq, I keep shipping, I get good at the politics, I learn to manage Roberto-types from a position of seniority. This is the legible path. Most of my ITESM cohort is on some version of this path. It is not bad. I am not unhappy on it.

The path I'm trying to open: MIT Media Lab masters. Two years, MIT. Probably ~80k tuition + living, partially funded if I'm lucky, fully self-funded plus loans if I'm not. Career break of 2 years. Then either a PhD (5-7 more years), or an industry research job (Anthropic, OpenAI research, FAIR, MSR, DeepMind), or back to industry IC at a research-flavored team (Adept-style, Glean-style). Or, the weird option, a full faculty pivot which I almost certainly will not pursue at 35-37.

The rumination is: which path is correct, given who I actually am, not who I want to be on Sundays.

What I know about myself, from the data of the past 18 months:
- I get bored when problems are solved. RouteOpt rust shipped to staging at 38ms p95 and I felt hollow within a week. Researchers report this hollowness as a feature, ICs report it as a bug.
- I read papers in my unstructured time. I have read more papers in 2026 than I read in all of 2014 in school.
- I do not actually love the IC craft for its own sake. I love the questions. I love the questions and I tolerate the craft as the price of getting the questions answered. This is the inverse of how my Brújula peers describe their relationship to engineering.
- I am bad at the politics. Not catastrophically — I survive Roberto — but I find it genuinely draining in a way my peers don't. Two engineers I know find office politics fun. They are senior engineers because they find it fun. I find it tedious.
- I have done two career pivots already (Kueski → Wizeline → Brújula) and each one felt vertiginous in the moment and obvious in retrospect. Pattern-match suggests this one is the same.
- I have ~45k USD saved. Plus 8k USD in student loans, manageable. I can afford one expensive year of MIT before I'd need to take loans. Two years is harder. Camila has stable income at Foster + Partners.
- The work that gives me genuine joy on a Tuesday afternoon is reading a paper, drafting a Mastodon thread, looking at the PostGIS crate I open-sourced and seeing it has 87 stars. Not the rust commit log. Not the prod metric dashboard. The thinking work.

What I don't know:
- Whether I will get in. Acceptance rate at the Media Lab masters is brutal.
- Whether I will love research once I am inside it. There is a non-zero chance I get to MIT, do my first semester, and discover I miss shipping. The IC romanticization theory.
- Whether I will hate being a student again at 33-34, surrounded by people younger than me. The status step-down.
- Whether Camila's career trajectory holds in Boston. Foster + Partners has a Boston office but no architect-of-her-level position open right now.
- Whether my parents will quietly worry. (They will. That's known. I can't optimize for it.)
- Whether I'm romanticizing 'research' the way I once romanticized 'consulting' before Wizeline disabused me of that romance in about six months.

The least-bad path forward, as far as I can tell tonight: assume I get in. If I do, take it. The downside of taking it and discovering I'm an IC at heart is two years and some money and a great story; the downside of not taking it is wondering for the next 30 years. Asymmetric regret. If I don't get in, apply again next cycle, refine the angle. If I don't get in twice, take the data seriously and reroute — maybe industry research at a frontier lab via the public-paper-and-cold-email route, or an internal research position at a larger company via networking.

The other-other-other consideration: what would I tell a friend in this exact situation? If Pablo came to me with this exact stack of facts and pros and cons, I'd tell him to go. I'd tell him the regret-asymmetry argument. I'd tell him the career-pivot pattern-match argument. I'd tell him to stop interrogating his own motives and let the application play out. I'd tell him you can always come back to industry but you cannot easily go back to 33 with a clean two-year window in front of you.

That's the answer. The advice I'd give a friend is the advice I should take. The interrogation of motives is a procrastination ritual. I have submitted the application. The only thing left to do is wait, and to live a normal life in the waiting.

The thing that helps me sleep tonight is: Camila and Pablo and Daniela all, separately, when I asked them about this, said 'you're going to do this.' Three independent reads. None of them are flatterers. None of them have anything to gain. If three people who know me well all see the same trajectory, the trajectory is probably real. I should probably stop interrogating my own motives and let the application play out.

The other thing that helps me sleep — the smaller thing — is the memory of Mama Lupita, who died in 2019, and how I never visited her in her last year because I was too busy building career capital at Wizeline. Career capital that I have now traded for the chance to do something I actually want. The trade was uneven on her end. I owe it to her to make it less uneven on mine.

The last thing — and this is the thing I keep coming back to when I imagine the rejection scenario — is that even if MIT says no, I will have done the work of the application, which has clarified my own thinking in a way nothing else has. The personal statement forced me to articulate what I actually want to study. That articulation will not disappear if MIT rejects me. It will inform the next application, the next side project, the next mid-life pivot in 2030. The application is itself a tool, separate from its outcome. Whatever February brings, I am 18 months smarter about my own work than I was in October 2024. That's a real win that the binary outcome cannot take from me.

I'll close the laptop and go to bed and try not to refresh the MIT portal one more time. I will fail at the second one.

 The career-pivot rumination is also being shaped by the texture of recent days. The post-application float, the chess board moment, Hugo at the party, the way Camila and I had the long talk last night. The future feels less abstract this week than it has in months. I can almost see two specific paths — the Boston version and the CDMX version — with enough texture that I'm picking up on the daily-life rhythms of each. Boston version: Cambridge winter, walking to MIT in a parka, Camila working on East Coast institutional projects, weekend trips to Cape Cod, learning to live without yerba mate access on every corner, Nube in snow boots (do they make those?). CDMX version: continuing in Brújula or moving to a different role, finally buying a small apartment, Sunday brunches at Doña Marta's, weekend bike rides to Xochimilco, kids in 2027-28, watching my parents age in 2-hour-flight reach. Both versions are full lives. Both versions include the same names — Camila, Nube, Mateo, my parents, Hugo, Pablo, Daniela, Profe. The cast is the constant. The setting is the variable. The setting matters but the cast matters more. If MIT says yes, the cast moves to a different stage. If MIT says no, the cast stays put. The cast doesn't change. The work of the next 40 years is the cast, not the stage. I keep coming back to this. I think it is the answer that lets me sleep tonight. The cast is the answer. The MIT decision is just about which stage. Either stage is a stage I can build a 40-year life on. Either stage will be a good stage with this cast. Going to bed.  The other variable I keep forgetting to factor: time-of-life regret asymmetry. At 33 with one year of clean two-year window in front of me, taking the masters has the lowest opportunity cost it will ever have. Five years from now I will likely have a child, more obligations, less mobility. Ten years from now the masters becomes essentially impossible. The optimal time to take the masters is now. Even if I get rejected this cycle, the optimal time to apply the second time is also now-ish. The window narrows. The pivot has to happen in this 24-month band or it doesn't happen. That's a real constraint and I should treat it as one.

 The other thing I want to record — and this is important for me to remember in case the rejection comes — is that the people who told me 'you are going to do this' all agreed on the trajectory but disagreed on the timeline. Camila thinks first try. Pablo thinks first try. Daniela thinks 'first or second try.' My dad thinks 'three tries was fine for me, will be fine for you.' My mom thinks 'whenever, mijo.' Lozano said 'the right cohort, eventually.' The signal is unanimous on direction and noisy on timing. The right move is to take the direction seriously and not over-fit to the timing.

Roberto pinged me on Slack today asking for a 'rough timeline for the prod rollout.' He's been asking variants of this question for three weeks. The honest answer is: Carla and I are baking observability into the rust service before we flip the traffic, Andrés is writing the migration runbook, and we'll be ready in 2-3 weeks. I gave him exactly that. He replied with 'can we tighten that to 1-2 weeks?' which is Roberto-speak for 'I want to tell my boss a smaller number.' I said 'we can tighten it to 1-2 weeks if we cut the canary phase, which I would not recommend.' He replied with a thumbs-up. That's how the conversation ended. This is the dance. This is what I am potentially trading for two years of grad school. There is some clarity in stating it that way. Carla messaged me right after, on a side channel, with the eye-roll emoji. She and I have a quiet alliance. Andrés is too junior to weigh in. I appreciate that she's been around long enough to recognize the dance for what it is. After Carla's eye-roll she asked if I want to grab a coffee Friday and 'talk about the next thing.' She doesn't know about the MIT app. I haven't told anyone at Brújula except Daniela for obvious reasons. I told Carla yes to the coffee but I haven't decided whether to tell her about MIT. She is the kind of person who would be supportive and discreet. She is also the kind of person who deserves the heads-up that the team's tech lead might be gone in 12 months. I'll think about it. Probably tell her over the coffee. Probably.

 The 2-3 week timeline is a real number. We need to bake in canary observability — the rust service is going to take 1% of traffic for 48 hours, then 10% for 48 hours, then 50% for 72 hours, then 100% rollover. Each step has a rollback runbook. Carla and I wrote the canary plan together over two long sessions in month 16. Andrés has been running the staging-load tests this week and the metrics look clean — 38ms p95 holding under sustained burst, no memory regression, no Tokio runtime starvation. The data is there for a confident rollout. Roberto wants the smaller number because his quarterly review is coming up and a rust-migration-completed bullet would make his slides nicer. I sympathize with his political incentives but I am not going to compromise the rollout for them. Carla agrees. Andrés is too junior to weigh in but I have explicitly told him that we ship on the canary plan timeline regardless of leadership pressure. He nodded gravely. He is becoming a senior engineer in front of my eyes.  I am going to draft a one-pager for Carla outlining the canary plan in writing. Even though we wrote it together, I want her to have a clean version she can hand off if I have to step away for any reason. Defensive engineering applied to my own relationship to my own job.

Camila and I are going to do a single check-in session with Dr. Muñoz next week. We paused therapy at 3 sessions back in month 8. We're not in crisis — actually the opposite, we want to do a check-in while things are good, before MIT decision news either way changes the whole landscape. The kid-timing conversation has been quietly humming under everything. Camila said she wants to talk about it with someone in the room who isn't us. I agreed. I think that's healthy. I think the version of me from a year ago would've avoided the session. The version of me now booked it. Dr. Muñoz responded to the email within 90 minutes which is faster than usual — I think she remembers us and was glad we reached out. We have a 6pm slot Tuesday next week. Camila put it in our shared calendar with a heart emoji. The first session was 11 months ago. Lifetime ago. Different couple. We're going to walk in there with the framework we've already roughed out — MIT outcome determines geographic next 2 years, kid timing follows from there with a 1-year buffer post-stability — and we're going to ask Dr. Muñoz to stress-test it. That's a different therapy use case than crisis-triage. I think it's the right one. I'm actually a little excited for the session, which is not a sentence I expected to ever type about therapy. Growth observable in real time.

 The other thing the Dr. Muñoz check-in is going to surface, I suspect, is the question of how Camila and I want to handle the 4 months of waiting. She has her own anxiety patterns around big-decision uncertainty — she does not spiral the way I do, she goes quiet and gets a little tense in the shoulders for a few days at a time, and then she calls her sister in São Paulo and the two of them go for a long walk on opposite continents and somehow this fixes her. My pattern is the 3am rumination. We are different patterns. The session might help us articulate to each other how to support the other one's pattern without trying to convert it. Dr. Muñoz is good at that exact kind of articulation. The first session 11 months ago helped us articulate the kid-timing fight in a way that turned it from a recurring crisis into a recurring conversation. I want to see her translate the 4-month-wait into a similar shape. It probably will not be a long session. We have homework, basically.  Camila's shoulders have been less tight this week. I can tell because she stopped doing the small head-tilt-to-the-right she does when her trapezius is locked up. Body language as a stress diagnostic. She does not know I read her like this. She would tease me if she knew.

going to bed. brain feels lighter than it has in months. weird sensation. enjoying it. the post-application float is a real thing. nube on the rug. camila reading. lights low. october 25 ends here. tomorrow is just sunday. that is allowed.

 The way the lamp on Camila's side throws shadow on the wall is suddenly something I'm noticing for the first time in months. The book she's reading is a Lispector — same one she gave me but in the original — and she's reading slowly with her finger under the line, mouthing the Portuguese. She caught me looking and smiled without saying anything. The fan is on low. Nube is doing the slow contented breathing that is the most reassuring sound I know. October 25, ten o'clock at night. Submitted yesterday. Survived today. Going to sleep tonight. The arc continues but the shape is gentler now. I will take it.  Phone in the other room. The rule holds tonight. Going.

Called my parents tonight. Mom answered, put me on speaker. Dad's angina has been quiet for a few weeks (he qualified this with 'so far,' which is his usual). Mom is fine, no more lump scares. I told them the application is in. Mom said 'mijo, qué orgullo' and her voice did the thing it does. Dad said 'remember it took me three tries to get into the masters in physics. Don't take rejection personally if it comes.' That was unexpected. He'd never told me that. He said it like it was nothing. We talked for 20 minutes about the chess board and Hugo's reaction at the party last week — they keep retelling it to each other apparently. I didn't realize how much it mattered to them until they kept circling back to it. Dad said again, in passing, 'don't forget mine in four years.' I told him I won't. I won't. Mom asked when we're coming up for Christmas. I said December 21 to December 28 — it's a longer trip than usual because we're trying to do Christmas with my family and then New Year's in CDMX with Hugo since he's flying back over. She said good, she's going to make tamales, and asked Camila what kind of pan dulce she wants. Camila yelled an answer from the other room. The call ended at 9:42. I sat with the phone for a minute before putting it down. Dad's three-tries-for-the-masters is a thing he has never mentioned in 33 years. I do not know what to do with the fact that he had to tell me about MIT for it to come up. I am going to call him tomorrow. Just to call him. Not for any reason.

 After we hung up I sat on the couch and stared at the wall for ten minutes. Dad's three-tries-for-the-masters had landed harder than anything else in the conversation. My dad got his masters in physics from UNAM in 1992 — I had always known this — and I had assumed he'd gotten in on the first try because the version of dad I grew up with always seemed to do things efficiently. Apparently not. Apparently he tried in 1989, was rejected. Tried in 1990, was rejected. Tried in 1991, accepted. He never told me. He told me tonight because his son was on the verge of his own masters application and dad wanted to make sure I had a shock absorber if rejection hit. Three tries. The man kept applying. The man also worked at a high-school physics-teacher salary the whole time, in a way I was not until tonight calibrating, and he applied three times. I do not deserve the version of this man that I am. I am going to call him tomorrow. Just to call him. Maybe I tell him I'm proud of him for trying three times. Maybe I just talk about Nube. Whatever lets the conversation be a conversation. I will call.  Mateo texted me 10 minutes after the call ended saying 'mom told me about dad's grad school three-tries thing.' He had not known either. We both have been somewhat re-meeting our father at age 33 and 28 respectively. There is more to him than we realized. There always is.

It's 3:11am. Awake again. Different flavor of insomnia tonight — not the pre-submission grind anymore, this is the post-submission spiral. The brain has nothing to grind against, and instead of resting it has decided to invent imaginary rejection emails. I have composed approximately seven of them in my head between 1:40 and now.

Let me just write them out so I can stop.

First imagined email: 'Dear Sebastián, thank you for your application to the MIT Media Lab. We received a record number of applications this cycle and unfortunately must inform you...' Standard form rejection. The kind of email that deletes the future. I imagine reading it on my phone at the Brújula office and having to maintain a poker face during stand-up. I imagine Camila's face when I tell her. I imagine the second-cycle apply, the iteration, the better-positioned-next-time speech.

Second imagined email: a more personal rejection. 'We were impressed by your application but felt the fit with our current research priorities was not strong.' This one is more painful because it implies they read it carefully. I imagine sending the email to Daniela and Pablo and Lozano and saying 'thanks for trying.'

Third imagined email: a waitlist. The torture variant. 'You have been placed on our waitlist...' Months of low-grade hope.

Fourth: an interview invitation. This one I let myself imagine for about 90 seconds before talking myself down because if I get attached to it I will be devastated when it doesn't come.

Fifth: an acceptance. I won't even type it because I'm superstitious.

Sixth: an acceptance with funding. I refuse to even hold the thought.

Seventh: a 'we'd like to invite you to apply to a different program' redirect. This one I genuinely have not been able to predict the emotional response to. It's Schrödinger's email.

The absurd part is the application was submitted four days ago. Decisions don't come for months. I am pre-grieving outcomes that aren't due until February. This is not a productive use of my brain at 3am.

What I know about my own pattern from the past 18 months: when I'm under-stimulated my brain invents stimulation. Insomnia peaks correlate not with stress, but with the absence of a clear task. Last year's insomnia peak was month 16 — the rust migration was about to ship and there was nothing left to do but wait. Same pattern now. Brain hates the void. Brain fills the void with imagined catastrophes.

Actionable thoughts at 3:14am:
- Trazodone if it gets worse. Vet uses it for Nube in storms. (Joke. Sort of.)
- The melatonin works for getting to sleep but not for staying asleep, which is the actual problem. Maybe ask Dr. Muñoz next week if she can refer me to a sleep specialist.
- The CBT-I app didn't stick because I am not a person who will use a phone app to fix my brain. Maybe try the actual workbook.
- Stop drinking yerba mate after 4pm. I keep saying this. I keep failing at it.
- Build a project. The brain needs a thing. The chess board took up 3 months of unstructured time and the pose-est before that. Without a project, the brain is feral. What's the next project? I don't know yet. Maybe something small. Maybe finally finish the hot-sauce recipe book I've been talking about for 3 years. Something that won't compete with potential MIT mental load if I get in.
- Write an actual email to Picard. Not asking for anything. Just acknowledging I'm in the applicant pool, briefly stating my background, and noting one specific paper of hers that influenced my thinking. Cold but warm. The kind of email you can send without expectation. I have been resisting this because it feels presumptuous. But the worst case is she doesn't read it; the best case is she reads it and remembers my name when she sees the application packet. Asymmetric upside. I'll draft it tomorrow when I'm not 3am-brained.

One more thing. The reason I'm awake. It's not actually the imagined rejections. It's that for the first time in eighteen months I do not have a thing to deliver. The arc that ran from October 2024 to October 2026 was a constant series of deliveries — funeral logistics, promo packet, post-mortem, rust migration milestones, the kid-timing conversation, the chess board, the personal statement. Always one more thing on Tuesday. Tuesday now is just Tuesday. The hollow that follows the delivery is what's keeping me up. The hollow is not bad. The hollow is just unfamiliar. I have to learn to live in it for a few months.

Another thing. The cortisol of the application was carrying real load. Now that the load is gone the body is asking 'what now' and not getting a satisfying answer. The body wants an answer. The body wants something to clench against. There is nothing for it to clench against. So the body is inventing things to clench against, which is what the imagined rejection emails are. They are the body's request for a load to bear. The healthy response is to give the body something else to bear — the gym, the hot sauce zine, the cutting board for Pablo, the cold email to Picard. Anything that's a real load instead of a phantom one. I'm going to make a list tomorrow. Cold-email Picard is on the list. The hot-sauce zine is on the list. Maybe even open the Lispector book Camila gave me and try to read three pages without using the dictionary. The list is the medicine. Without the list I will keep inventing rejection emails for the next 90 days and Camila will leave me. (She will not leave me. She has the patience of a saint. But the principle stands.)

Doña Marta starts the oven in 49 minutes. The smell of hot bread will be a cruel taunt at 4am. I'm going to lie back down. If I'm awake at 4am writing this from my phone again I'll know the spiral is winning. If I'm asleep at 4am I'll know the hollow is teaching me something. Either way the bread will be there at 5.

 The other thing I want to write down before I close this. The night feeling. The 3am light. The fact that I am alone with my brain and a phone and a glass of water and the dog snoring downstairs. There is a particular kind of solitude in 3am insomnia that does not exist at any other hour. It is not the solitude of a quiet morning. It is not the solitude of a long bus ride. It is the solitude of being unable to sleep while the people you love are asleep three meters away. They are right there and they are unreachable. The phone is in the other room. The brain is the only company. The brain is bad company at 3am. The brain at 3am is not the same brain as the 11am brain. The 3am brain has a different relationship to risk, to memory, to identity. The 3am brain is a different person in a meaningful way. I have to remember this when I read these notes back tomorrow. The 3am Sebastián who wrote about imagined rejection emails is not the 11am Sebastián who is going to read those notes. The 3am Sebastián is closer to a 17-year-old version of me than to the version of me that ships rust services. The 3am Sebastián thinks in catastrophes. The 11am Sebastián thinks in plans. They are different people. I should not give the 3am Sebastián decision-making authority over things the 11am Sebastián is responsible for. The notes from 3am are a diary of the 3am person, not a directive for the 11am person. I am going to carry this distinction with me. The thing the 11am person should do tomorrow is read the notes, make a list of the actionable items, and then archive the rest. The catastrophes go in the archive. The actions go on the list. The 11am person executes the list. The 3am person takes the night off.

 Doña Marta starts in 28 minutes. The bread will smell like reassurance. The light through the kitchen window will turn soft amber. Camila will wake at 7 and find me on the couch with notes-app open and will not say anything except to bring me coffee. This is the post-3am-rant ritual. We have done it many times. The morning forgives the night. Going to lie back down and try one more time. If it works, great. If it doesn't, I have notes for the 11am Sebastián to read.  The phone is buzzing with a notification from a Mastodon thread I started two days ago about the migration. Some Tokio core dev replied with a useful pointer about the multi-thread runtime tuning. I am not going to read it tonight. I am going to read it at 11am tomorrow when 11am-Sebastián is back in command. The 3am Sebastián is not allowed to read technical content. Bad ratio of catastrophe-fuel to insight at this hour.

Slept maybe 5 hours after last night's spiral. Up early, walked Nube, made coffee, told Camila what my brain was doing at 3am and she said 'mi amor, you've been doing this for months, can we make a rule that you write me a note instead of writing your brain a five-page essay?' I said yes. The rule is: when I wake at 3am, I tap her on the shoulder, she says 'go back to sleep,' I go back to sleep. Or try to. We'll see if it works. The 'go back to sleep' instruction from someone you love at 3am has a different weight than the same instruction from your own brain. We'll see if my body recognizes the difference. I'm willing to try the experiment. We tested it tonight at midnight as a dry run — I was about to start spiraling on whether I should have written a different second paragraph in the personal statement, I tapped her shoulder, she said 'go back to sleep' without opening her eyes, and somehow it worked. The brain just turned off the loop. Five years of sleeping next to someone has trained my nervous system to recognize her voice as a parasympathetic signal. I had not known this. Now I know. Going to deploy more aggressively.

 The other thing I want to write down — Camila's been quietly doing this for me for years and I'd never named it. She has a way of providing low-key parasympathetic regulation through her presence alone. Five years of sleeping next to her, eating across from her, walking next to her have apparently trained my nervous system to drop into a calmer state when she's near. I had not understood this as a real physiological thing. Tonight's experiment confirmed it: her voice at midnight, half-asleep, saying 'go back to sleep,' did something my own internal narration could never do. I think this is part of what long-term partnership is for. Not just companionship. Co-regulation. We are each other's nervous-system anchors. I am going to thank her tomorrow morning. She will brush it off. She will brush it off in a way that confirms the thing.

 We tested it tonight at midnight as a dry run. I was about to start spiraling. I tapped her shoulder. She said the four words. I went back to sleep. Five years of conditioning is a real biological asset. Going to deploy it more. Camila has agreed to be the parasympathetic intervention without being grumpy about it. She did say I owe her one extra long Sunday morning of laptop-free coffee per shoulder-tap. I have agreed. The exchange rate is fair.  I want to remember that the rule we tested tonight worked partly because I had explicit consent to use her presence as a regulator. The consent makes it different from just nudging her awake. The consent makes it a tool we both own. We will calibrate. The rule is provisional. Real partnership.

Camila and I had the long talk tonight. The one about the next 5 years, conditional on the various MIT outcomes. We've been circling it for weeks. I'd been avoiding it because I didn't want to make plans on top of an unsubmitted application; she'd been respecting the avoidance. After the submission and the party for her dad and a quiet Sunday it just kind of arrived naturally — we were on the couch, Nube was asleep on the rug, it was 8pm, and Camila said 'OK, can we talk?'

The scenarios.

Scenario A: I get into MIT. We move to Boston for two years (her firm has a Boston office, she'd lateral over, possibly with a small comp drop, possibly not, depends on what's open in Q1). She said tonight she's checked informally with two of her colleagues already and there's at least one open spot at the right level. We rent in Cambridge. Nube comes. Apartment will be smaller, climate worse, food worse, but the cohort and the city would be a real adventure. We'd be there from 2027 to 2029. She wants to be open about the Boston option not being a sacrifice — she said tonight, with some emphasis, that she's been wanting an excuse to work the Boston office because the firm's American institutional projects are stronger there. I didn't know that. I'd been carrying around guilt about asking her to move and apparently the guilt was misplaced. She wants the move. That changed something for me. I had been bracing for a conversation that started 'OK what would I be giving up' and instead it started 'OK what would I be getting.' Different shape. Different posture. I have to recalibrate my own internal narrative because for six months I had been quietly running the ledger of how much I'd be costing her, and the ledger turns out to be wrong in significant ways.

Scenario B: I don't get in. We stay in CDMX. I either re-apply next cycle or pivot to industry research via a different door. We either rent or buy in CDMX in the next 18 months. She mentioned, gently, that her firm has been talking about her potentially leading a new internal program in 2027 that would anchor her here for 3-4 years. So Scenario B is actually attractive too. Not a downgrade. Just a different shape of life. I noticed, sitting there, that for the first time in two years I had two genuinely good scenarios in front of me, not one good and one consolation. Both versions of the next five years are appealing. That's a luxury I don't think I've ever had before.

The kid timing. We talked about it. Honestly, we talked about it more clearly tonight than we have in any of the three couples-therapy sessions or any of the dozen private conversations since month 7. She said: in Scenario A, kid timing is post-MIT, so 2029-2030, when I'm 36-37 and she's 33-34. Late but not unreasonable. In Scenario B, the timing is more flexible, and she'd want to start trying in 2027. Both windows are workable for her. Both windows scare me different amounts, and I told her that, and she said that's fair. We agreed: we don't lock the kid timing tonight. We lock the framework. The framework is: MIT outcome determines geographic next 2 years; kid timing follows from there with a 1-year buffer post-stability.

The other thing — and this is where I got emotional — she said something about her dad and the chess board last week, about how watching him receive the gift made her think harder about what we want our 70th to look like. She said: 'I want to build the kind of life where, at 70, you're crying over something someone made you with their hands.' I don't know if I'm getting that quote exactly right. But it landed. We're not on a sprint. We're on a 40-year project. She also said, almost as an aside, that the chess board moment had clarified something for her about what kind of family she wants — a family where the children grow up making things for the grandparents. A family where the value gets transmitted in objects you can touch. I had not heard her say something like that before. We did not directly talk about kids again after she said it. But it sat there.

The Bogotá decline came up briefly. She thanked me again for it. I told her I was actually glad I'd declined because if I'd gone to Bogotá I wouldn't have had the bandwidth to apply to MIT, and the Bogotá comp would've been a golden handcuff. She said 'I told you so' which she had not, in fact, told me. I let it pass.

Money came up too — briefly, calmly, more like an inventory than an argument. Scenario A: my 45k saved + her continued salary + partial loans gets us through Boston without catastrophic damage. Scenario B: we'd be in a position to buy a small place in CDMX within 18 months, possibly with a down payment we can pull together by mid-2027. We've been together five years and we have never sat down to do a money inventory like this. It took fifteen minutes. The number was less scary than I expected. We have more runway than my anxiety has been telling me we have.

Dr. Muñoz check-in next week now has a different tenor. We're not bringing in a list of fights to triage. We're bringing in a framework to test. That's a healthier use of the session. It might even be a short session. The pause we took at 3 sessions back in month 8 was because we'd run out of fights to triage and didn't know what to do with the time. Now we have something to do with the time — stress-test the framework. Different therapy use case. Probably the right one.

Going to bed feeling like I have a plan instead of a fog. The fog has been part of the insomnia. I think tonight I sleep through. We left the conversation at around 10pm, brushed our teeth, got into bed, and Camila put her head on my chest and said 'this is going to be a good five years either way.' I believed her. I believe her. The thing I will remember about tonight is not the scenarios. It's the way she said it. It's the certainty. It's that she has done the math and concluded the math is good either way, and she shared the conclusion with me, and now I get to carry that conclusion around with me into whatever February brings.

One more thing. As we were drifting off she said quietly 'whatever happens, thank you for trying.' I said thank you for trying what. She said 'just thank you for trying. Most people don't apply. You applied.' I lay there in the dark thinking about that for a long time. There is a non-zero moral weight to having done the thing. Even if MIT says no, the application itself is a moral act — a public statement that I take this seriously enough to bet on it. Camila sees that. I think Hugo sees that. Pablo definitely sees it. The version of me that did not apply does not deserve the version of me that did. I am glad to be the version that did.

 One last thing about tonight that I want to log before sleep. I had been carrying around, for about six months, a quiet image of how this conversation might go badly. The bad version was: Camila reluctantly agrees to consider Boston as a sacrifice, the kid timing becomes a tense subtopic, the money inventory exposes more anxiety than I want to admit, and we both go to bed with that particular post-fight numbness. I had been bracing for the bad version for so long that I had not let myself imagine what the good version would feel like. The actual conversation tonight — the slow, calm, genuinely-curious conversation about both scenarios — was a third thing I had not prepared for. It was not the bad version. It was also not the bright-eyed-let's-do-it good version. It was the careful-shared-life version. We had data. We had been thinking about this independently for months. We brought our independent thinking to the couch and we crossed the data with care. The crossing produced a framework. The framework will get tested in February when the MIT decision comes. The framework will get tested again when we eventually decide on kid timing. The framework will get tested in 5 years when I finish either the masters or whatever Plan B becomes. The framework is robust because it was built carefully, and it was built carefully because we have been together five years and we have learned to build frameworks before crises rather than during them. This is a thing married couples (or married-equivalent couples) get good at if they're paying attention. We are paying attention. We have learned. We are different people from the people we were when we started couples therapy at month 7. Dr. Muñoz will see this difference next week and will, I think, smile. The growth is real. The framework is real. The next five years will be good either way. I am going to sleep now. Camila is already asleep. The apartment is quiet. The chess board is on the dining table with the Italian opening still on it. The arc that started in São Paulo with a funeral 18 months ago is closing tonight in Roma Norte with a calm shared plan. There is a kind of symmetry in that. Funerals start things. Plans end things. The 18 months had a shape after all.  The conversation also touched on something we had not directly named in five years. Camila said tonight that she had quietly been measuring whether I would ever choose myself over what is expedient, and that the MIT application was the first observation she had where the answer was clearly yes. She said she had been waiting to see this without telling me she was waiting. That landed hard. I did not know I was being watched on this axis. Apparently I was. Apparently I had passed. The pass was not announced. The pass was just absorbed into the framework. The framework now assumes I am the kind of partner who chooses the harder version of myself. That's a heavy assumption to live up to. I am going to live up to it.

 The thing I want my future self to know about tonight is that the conversation made me happier than the submission made me. The submission was a closing of a chapter. The conversation was the opening of a different one. The next 5 years are now a shared plan, not a private hope. That changes the texture of the days. I had not realized how lonely the private-hope version had been until the shared-plan version replaced it. Loneliness ended tonight in a small specific way. Going to sleep with that.

 Camila is asleep already. Nube has migrated from the rug to the foot of the bed, where she has decided she is now allowed to be on Saturdays after sunset. I did not negotiate this.
